EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture moving north across northern Arizona and southern Utah brought heavy rain from thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across Yavapai County between September 2 and 3. Here are some 24 hour amounts (in inches) ending at noon on September 3: 11 miles SW of Prescott 1.28, 3 miles northwest of Camp Verde 1.25, 2 miles southwest of McGuireville 1.25,  and 2 miles southwest of Cottonwood 0.93.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure moving eastward across California brought a plume of deep moisture to Arizona as well. The synoptic-scale ascent from the trough combined with the moisture allowed for periods of heavy rain over Arizona March 15 and 16.  Surface dewpoints were similar to summer time...in the 40s and 50s. The heavy rainfall, high snow levels, and deep snow pack combined to produce rapid snow melt and significant runoff into area waterways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive flooding was reported along Dry Beaver Creek across Kimberlys Way (150 feet wide). The river gauge upstream reported a 4 foot rise. The creek crested around midnight on March 16 at about 4.5 feet above the creek bed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Granite Creek Gauge showed water rising from 6.00 feet around 400 AM MST March 15 to a crest of 8.00 feet at midnight on March 16.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SANTA CRUZ RIVER AND ALTAR VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft strengthened over Arizona and created a period of very hot temperatures August 25-30 across southeast Arizona. High temperatures were 6 to 14 degrees above normal causing the Moderate to Major Heat Risk categories to be reached and numerous high temperature records to be established. A total of eleven heat-related fatalities were reported by the Pima County Medical Examiner.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures of 7 to 14 degrees above normal caused the Major Heat Risk category to be reached across southeast Pima County, south central Pinal County and the lower elevations of Santa Cruz County August 27-29. Record highs included 111 degrees at Picacho Peak; 108 at Sasabe; and 104 at the Nogales Airport, all on August 29.  While not a record, Picacho Peak reached 112 degrees on the 28th. The Pima County Medical Examiner determined that heat was the primary cause of death for two individuals found in the desert of southeast Pima County (one on the 28th about 8 miles southwest of Three Points and one on the 29th just east of the Baboquivari Mountains at 3800 ft near Elkhorn Ranch).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures of 7 to 11 degrees above normal caused the Major Heat Risk category to be reached across eastern Pima County August 27-30. Heat was found to be a contributing factor in the death of four individuals outdoors in Tucson during the period. Two were found outdoors and also experienced a complicating factor of drug toxicity. Three had complicating factors of drug toxicity and one suffered from other health conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved across the southwestern U.S. on the 1st, delivering moderate to locally heavy rainfall activity as well as embedded thunderstorm activity. Moisture values were well above normal for early January, with Precipitable water values ranged between 0.8-1. There was also some instability present, with Mixed Layer CAPE values between 100-500 J/KG, resulting in the formation of thunderstorms. The combination of the favorable moisture and instability parameters along with the strong dynamical forcing from the low pressure system resulted in very favorable conditions for a widespread swath of moderate to locally heavy rainfall to affect most of south-central Arizona. Rainfall amounts on average were under an inch across the lower deserts, however, across the higher terrain amounts were significantly higher, between 1-3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Floodwater out of Whitlow Canyon led to the closure of Silver King Rd. The employee from Queen Valley Fire Department said it was the highest flow he has ever seen. A Pinal County Flood Control streamflow gauge on Queen Creek at El Camino Viejo measured peak flow around 7 feet. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving across the western states resulted in waves of rainfall across the region during the day on the 15th. A round of widespread light to locally moderate rainfall activity was observed during the morning hours followed by a more significant round with thunderstorms during the evening hours in association with a cold front. Moisture levels associated with this low pressure system were in the 99th percentile for mid-March, signifying that environment was favorable for heavier rainfall rates to materialize. As a result of the heavier rainfall activity observed during the evening hours, flooding was observed across the Wickenburg area in northwestern Maricopa County, where over an inch of total rainfall was observed. Flooding was also observed along the low-water crossings that intersect the Hassayampa River, which runs from north to south along the western third of Maricopa County. Strong storms were also observed during the afternoon hours across southwestern Arizona, producing isolated gusts around 60 mph in the Yuma area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Patton Road between 293rd Ave and Douglas Ranch Rd was closed due to flooding from the overflow of the Hassayampa River. A Maricopa County Flood Control District Gauge located 11 miles upstream registered flows going above alarm stage, which is 9.0 feet, at 21:50 MST on 3/15, peaking at 9.81 feet (2011 cfs) at 23:30 MST. The flow went below alarm stage at 10:58 MST on 3/16. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The exceptionally deep and cold low pressure system moved across northern Arizona February 21 through early February 23. This storm brought high to very high winds, heavy snow, blowing snow, very cold temperatures, and low elevation rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow was reported near Pinetop-Lakeside by 200 PM. Ten to twelve inches of snow was reported 2 miles north of McNary by 300 PM with drifts 2 to 2.5 feet deep. The Show Low area received around 9 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong longwave trough digging through The Great Basin brought more moisture to Arizona in addition to the remnant moisture from a weak storm that just move across the state.  This allowed for precipitation to start the morning of February 14 across the favored upslope-forced areas along the southward facing terrain. ||During the afternoon of 14 February, an intense band of heavy precipitation moved across northern Arizona and lasted through the night. Snowfall rates of 1-2 inch/hour were seen over the higher terrain, especially from Flagstaff to Happy Jack to Forest Lakes.||Very cold temperatures were also observed with this storm with snow levels down to around 3500 feet by the morning of 15 February. ||High winds were also experienced with this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports from around the Western Mogollon Rim include (in inches): Doney Park 15.8, Kachina Village 14.8, Flagstaff 12.2, Bellemont (NWS Flagstaff) 11.6,  Munds Park 11.0, 3 SW Williams 10, and Parks 8.8.  Seventeen inches of snow fell at 10,800 feet about 10 miles NNW of Flagstaff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a major winter storm moved east of Arizona, a second winter storm moved across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine to ten inches of new snow fell in the Williams area. A report from COCORAHS had 13.5 inches of snow 5 miles south-southwest of Parks. Nine inches of snow fell one mile west-southwest of the Flagstaff Mall. Reports from Kachina Village were that 8.5 to 11 inches of new snow fell there.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a major winter storm moved east of Arizona, a second winter storm moved across the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eleven inches of snow fell near Desert View in the eastern part of The Grand Canyon National Park South Rim. Grand Canyon Village reported 8.5 inches of new snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak gust occurred at the Kingman Airport (KIGM) ASOS.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twenty-four inches of snow fell at 6500 feet in Hualapai Mountain Park. At lower elevations, two crashes involving snowy and icy roads resulted in a total of 15 injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds ahead of the same storm system that brought heavy snow to California caused brief dust storm conditions near Kingman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dust storm conditions reduced visibility to 50 feet on Highway 66 between Kingman and Valle Vista.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two lightning caused fires started at nearly the same time on July 19 on either side of Interstate 10 west of Dragoon in Cochise County. Both fires were spread by thunderstorm winds on July 20. The Robles Fire to the south of I-10, which consumed 3000 acres, was contained on July 22 with fire suppression costs tallying over $600,000. The Adams Peak Fire, which consumed 2232 acres to the north of I-10, was contained on July 23 with fire suppression costs of $500,000.

The Adams Peak Fire, which consumed 2232 acres to the north of I-10, was contained on July 23 with fire suppression costs of $500,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving across the west coast in combination with well above normal moisture levels for mid March standards with values above the 99th percentile resulted in waves of light to moderate rainfall activity across the region. The heaviest rainfall was observed across the areas to the north and east of Phoenix, where multiple inches of rain were observed. Given that the flows along rivers, small streams, creeks, and washes were elevated due to previous precipitation activity from previous storms and with the additional rainfall that fell, the water levels rose above alarm stage, resulting in flooding along low-water crossings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to MCDOT, the unbridged Agua Fria River crossing along Lower Buckeye Road between 127th Avenue and 4th Street was closed due to flooding. No damage or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving across the western states resulted in waves of rainfall across the region during the day on the 15th. A round of widespread light to locally moderate rainfall activity was observed during the morning hours followed by a more significant round with thunderstorms during the evening hours in association with a cold front. Moisture levels associated with this low pressure system were in the 99th percentile for mid-March, signifying that environment was favorable for heavier rainfall rates to materialize. As a result of the heavier rainfall activity observed during the evening hours, flooding was observed across the Wickenburg area in northwestern Maricopa County, where over an inch of total rainfall was observed. Flooding was also observed along the low-water crossings that intersect the Hassayampa River, which runs from north to south along the western third of Maricopa County. Strong storms were also observed during the afternoon hours across southwestern Arizona, producing isolated gusts around 60 mph in the Yuma area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of Jack Burden Rd in Wickenburg closed due to flooding. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late season winter storm moved across the Desert Southwest during the afternoon and evening on the 1st, lasting through the early morning hours on the 2nd. Strong winds were observed out ahead of an associated cold front with gusts in excess of 40 mph observed. Moderate to locally heavy rains associated with the cold front were also observed with most areas in and around the Phoenix metro observing between 0.5 to 1. This rainfall was enough to result in some elevated flows across northern Maricopa County near the Carefree and Cave Creek areas. Behind the cold front, a very cold air mass was pulled down into the region changing the rain to snow across areas mainly above 2000 feet during the overnight hours of the 1st into the 2nd, resulting in measurable snowfall accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: North Old Stage Road at the Cave Creek River crossing was closed due to flooding. No damages or injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Maricopa County Flood Control District Gauge at Casandro Wash in Wickenburg registered flows going above alarm stage, which is 2.2 feet, at 20:03 MST. This likely contributed to flooding along the inflow to Casandro Dam as well as small road crossings that intersect the wash. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The human caused Williams Fire started on April 3 in the Hereford area in Cochise County.  The wildfire was driven by wind gusts of 45 mph, spread northeast in dry grass, and destroyed two residences and three outbuildings near the intersection of Williams and Waters Rds. The fire caused evacuations of several residences and the closure of State Route 92. A total of 1320 acres were burned. Forward progression was stopped on April 4. Fire suppression costs tallied $90,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure continued to create hot temperatures 2 to 8 degrees above normal July 30-31 in parts of southeast Arizona and caused the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached. A total of 5 heat-related fatalities were reported by the Pima County Medical Examiner in Pima and Cochise counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures 2 to 4 degrees above normal caused the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached in the Tucson Metro area where temperatures topped out between 98 and 102. The Pima County Medical Examiner determined heat to be the primary cause of death for one individual who was found in the car in which he was living in southwest Tucson. Heat was found to be a contributing factor in the death of another person who died in their Oro Valley home in which the A/C unit was either broken or turned off. Heart disease was a complicating factor in this fatality.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures 3 to 5 degrees above normal caused the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached in the Sierra Vista area where temperatures reached the mid to upper 90s. The Pima County Medical Examiner determined heat to be the primary cause of death for an elderly man who died on a hiking trail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The lightning caused Gallineta Fire started July 23 in the southern Baboquivari Mountains in southeast Pima County. Due to windy and dry conditions, the fire quickly consumed 1601 acres before full containment was achieved on July 25.  Fire suppression costs tallied $400,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moving across the west coast in combination with well above normal moisture levels for mid March standards with values above the 99th percentile resulted in waves of light to moderate rainfall activity across the region. The heaviest rainfall was observed across the areas to the north and east of Phoenix, where multiple inches of rain were observed. Given that the flows along rivers, small streams, creeks, and washes were elevated due to previous precipitation activity from previous storms and with the additional rainfall that fell, the water levels rose above alarm stage, resulting in flooding along low-water crossings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Desert Hills Drive and 19th Ave were both closed due to flooding from Skunk Creek. A Maricopa County Flood Control District stream gauge showed water levels briefly rising above alarm stage, which is 2.2 feet. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late season winter storm moved across the Desert Southwest during the afternoon and evening on the 1st, lasting through the early morning hours on the 2nd. Strong winds were observed out ahead of an associated cold front with gusts in excess of 40 mph observed. Moderate to locally heavy rains associated with the cold front were also observed with most areas in and around the Phoenix metro observing between 0.5 to 1. This rainfall was enough to result in some elevated flows across northern Maricopa County near the Carefree and Cave Creek areas. Behind the cold front, a very cold air mass was pulled down into the region changing the rain to snow across areas mainly above 2000 feet during the overnight hours of the 1st into the 2nd, resulting in measurable snowfall accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A four-inch diameter tree limb snapped in the area of 50th street and Chandler Boulevard in Ahwatukee. No injuries were reported.

CZ_NAME: LITTLE COLORADO RIVER VALLEY IN APACHE COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved through The Great Basin and brought strong and damaging winds across northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds moved across the Little Colorado River Valley in Apache County from around 400 PM to around 600 PM.  The highest wind gust observed were 62 MPH at both Petrified Forest National Park (423 PM) and Springerville Airport (515 PM).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved through The Great Basin and brought strong and damaging winds across northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind was observed across the Western Mogollon Rim during the afternoon.  Some of the peak wind gusts observed were (in MPH): Williams Airport 62 at 315 PM, Flagstaff Airport 59 at 412 PM,  Arizona Snowbowl Pumphouse 58 250 PM (9,969 feet elevation).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure brought warm weather which caused melting snow and flooding. Some of the flooding was exacerbated by piles of snow blocking drainages ditches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rio de Flag overflowed along Dupont Avenue, Ellery Avenue, and Riles Street due to melting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure brought warm weather which caused melting snow and flooding. Some of the flooding was exacerbated by piles of snow blocking drainages ditches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Schultz Creek overflowed and impacted homes and apartments along Stevanna Way in the Coconino Estates area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Melting snow in the Chuska Mountains caused flooding in the Chinle area. The flood warning was downgraded to a flood advisory on April 30 at the request of the local Emergency Manager.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Navajo Nation Emergency Manager reported flooding in Chinle on the Chinle Creek early on April 23. The flood warning was downgraded to an advisory on April 30. The emergency operations center was staffed until May 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Anomalous snowfall across the high terrain of Arizona as well as lower elevation rainfall led to greater than normal runoff into the Salt and Verde watersheds. As a result of this, dam releases were conducted at multiple locations in these watersheds, leading to heightened flows in these rivers. Upstream releases led to additional releases from Granite Reef Dam, which initially began to cause impacts on the 4th when releases from the dam increased above 1,000 cfs. Granite Reef Dam releases continued to increase going through the month of March and at one point during the month was releasing nearly 40,000 cfs. Releases from this dam led to downstream flow in the normally dry Salt River, which runs through the Phoenix metro. The anomalous runoff into the Salt and Verde watersheds and the resultant dam releases led to numerous impacts along the Salt, Verde, and Gila Rivers, including multiple unbridged river crossings being flooded, multiple water rescues, and one recreational fatality. Flow in the Salt River continued downstream into the Gila River, causing more impacts along the way down to Painted Rock Dam. Releases continued through the remainder of the month and into April, resulting in prolonged impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to MCDOT, the unbridged Salt River crossing along 67th Avenue between Broadway Road and Southern Avenue was closed due to flooding beginning on the 12th and continued into April. Timing based on water releases from Granite Reef Dam.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maricopa County Sheriff's deputies received a call around 1145 MST on the 18th of a reported drowning after an 81-year-old man fell out of his kayak and didn't resurface. The incident occurred near the Pebble Beach area along the Salt River. Other kayakers on the river pulled the man out and administered first aid but were not successful. The man was pronounced dead at the scene. Approximately 20 minutes later, a family kayaking downstream on the Salt River near the Phon D Sutton Recreation Area had to be rescued after their kayak capsized. The family managed to stay afloat by holding onto some bushes and trees. All of the family members were safely rescued via helicopter by the Maricopa County Sheriff's Office. Ongoing dam releases were resulting in strong currents in the river at the time of these incidents. Event timing is based on downstream Granite Reef Dam releases.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed and moved northwest across southeast Arizona in a pattern than resembled the monsoon. Some thunderstorms produced wind damage in the Tucson Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm gust to 76 mph was recorded at Muleshoe Ranch RAWS in northwest Cochise County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landspout was reported near Chino Valley and was on the ground for one to two            minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably deep upper low moved across the Great Basin on April 4 allowing a cold and dry airmass to settle into Arizona overnight. In the Sulphur Springs Valley of northern Cochise and southern Graham counties, temperatures remained below 20 degrees for several hours from midnight to 7 am with a low temperature of 14 degrees. This resulted in major damage to the local fruit and nut crops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the Sulphur Springs Valley of northern Cochise and southern Graham counties, temperatures remained below 20 degrees for several hours from midnight to 7 am with a low temperature of 14 degrees. This late season hard freeze caused the total loss of the cherry and peach crops with a 90 percent loss of the apple and pear crops as the fruit was in full bloom at the time. Early crop pistachios were entirely lost.

While attempting to cross the flooded roadway, the recreational vehicle was swept off the road. The incident occurred around 2100 MST on the 30th at the unbridged El Mirage Road crossing that goes through the normally dry Gila River between Southern Avenue and Indian Springs Road. The two people were safely rescued by the fire department via helicopter. No injuries were reported. Event timing is based on Granite Reef Dam releases.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Complex of showers and thunderstorms moved eastward across central Arizona in the early morning hours south of the Mogollon Rim. Another wave of low pressure moved across Arizona for the second day in a row. Low level moisture transport resulted  organized convection along and south of the Mogollon Rim.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The grave gauge on the Verde River at Clarksdale showed a rapid 3 foot rise.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures in the mid to upper 20s with wind chills near 20 degrees resulted in cold that was 15 to 20 degrees below the normal low temperature for February 6. The Pima County Medical Examiner determined that hypothermia was the primary cause of death for a homeless individual who had ingested methamphetamines and was found outdoors in downtown Tucson. The individual was transported to a hospital, but died later that day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually moist pattern for mid-May was in place over Arizona with precipitable water values around 200-250% of normal. This allowed for the development of strong thunderstorms with heavy rain and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Yavapai County Alert gauge on Black Canyon Creek showed about a two foot rise at 1040 PM.  A downstream gauge (Auga Fria--AFRA3) showed a rise at 200 AM on April 20. The river remained elevated through the evening of May 23.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving across the western states resulted in waves of rainfall across the region during the day on the 15th. A round of widespread light to locally moderate rainfall activity was observed during the morning hours followed by a more significant round with thunderstorms during the evening hours in association with a cold front. Moisture levels associated with this low pressure system were in the 99th percentile for mid-March, signifying that environment was favorable for heavier rainfall rates to materialize. As a result of the heavier rainfall activity observed during the evening hours, flooding was observed across the Wickenburg area in northwestern Maricopa County, where over an inch of total rainfall was observed. Flooding was also observed along the low-water crossings that intersect the Hassayampa River, which runs from north to south along the western third of Maricopa County. Strong storms were also observed during the afternoon hours across southwestern Arizona, producing isolated gusts around 60 mph in the Yuma area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Yuma Auxiliary Airfield Station measured a 60 mph gust.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SANTA CRUZ RIVER AND ALTAR VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong late season storm system swept through southeast Arizona resulting in strong winds, followed by snow which reached the desert and valley floors of southeast Arizona. Heavy snow of around a foot in depth accumulated in the high country, but even some locations down to 3500 feet received four inches of snow causing travel difficulties. Even parts of the Tucson Metro area saw 1 to 3 inches of snow, although accumulations there were mostly limited to non-paved areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 4 of snow fell in the Patagonia area with gusts as high as 60 mph immediately preceding the changeover to snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The human-caused West Hemp Fire started June 27 on the grounds of Fort Huachuca when the blades of a lawn mower struck a rock. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure moving eastward across California brought a plume of deep moisture to Arizona as well. The synoptic-scale ascent from the trough combined with the moisture allowed for periods of heavy rain over Arizona March 15 and 16.  Surface dewpoints were similar to summer time...in the 40s and 50s. The heavy rainfall, high snow levels, and deep snow pack combined to produce rapid snow melt and significant runoff into area waterways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Verde River Gauge near Clarkdale started raising rapidly around 1000 AM March 15 from around 8.00 feet to a crest of around 15.8 feet by 1100 PM MST on March 15. The Verde River dropped back to around 8.0 feet by 900 AM on March 18.  The Verde River in Camp Verde started to rise rapidly around 500 PM on March 15 from 11.8 feet. The crest was at 20.8 feet at 300 March 16 and the river returned to 11.8 feet by 900 PM on March 19.  The Verde River below Tangle Creek began to rise rapidly around 500 PM from 12.0 feet.  The crest was around 700 PM at 20.3 feet and slowly fell back to 12.0 feet by 400 PM by 400 PM March 19.||Homes were flooded along Bates Drive in Cottonwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first in a series of disturbances moved across Arizona from March 20 to 21. This caused widespread snow showers to develop.  The heaviest snow was over the higher terrain in upslope areas. This storm also delivered strong and damaging winds. The higher snow levels caused significant melting of lower elevation snow and flooding. The flooding took several days to flow down major rivers in northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 377 between Heber and Holbrook was closed due to flooding at mile post 13 around 900 AM on March 22. Seymour's Crossing was flooded the night of March 22. A vehicle was swept downstream for about an hour. The occupants were able to reach the bank but, were separated. The woman's body was found the next day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures 3 to 6 degrees above normal were experienced in the lower elevations of Pima, Santa Cruz, Graham, Greenlee and southeast Pinal counties June 24-30, with very hot temperatures 5 to 11 degrees above normal high temperatures breaking records in a few locations June 25-27. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for two individuals found in the desert, one in Santa Cruz County and another on the Tohono O'odham Nation. Heat was determined to be a contributing factor in the death of five individuals in Tucson.

Tucson International Airport topped out at 105 degrees. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was a contributing factor in the death of one individual found in a homeless encampment in Tucson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the subtropical high pressure situated across central New Mexico, a southeasterly wind flow was established across Arizona. Thunderstorm activity developed across portions of southeast AZ during the afternoon hours. MLCAPE between 500-1000 J/KG as well as Downdraft CAPE near 2000 J/KG aided in creating a favorable environment for the thunderstorm activity to result in strong downburst winds. The first thunderstorm activity to develop across portions of southeast AZ resulted in the development of a strong outflow boundary, responsible for dense blowing dust conditions across Pinal and southern Maricopa Counties as well as sparking additional storms that affected portions of the Phoenix metro area. With the thunderstorms that moved across the Phoenix metro area, there were several reports of tree damage as well as structural damage to several homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branch down in the area of East Boston Street and South Santan Village Parkway in Gilbert. Tree branch is estimated to be three inches in diameter, breaking the windows of parked car. Wind gusts were estimated to be 55-60 mph. No injuries were reported.

MLCAPE between 500-1000 J/KG as well as Downdraft CAPE near 2000 J/KG aided in creating a favorable environment for the thunderstorm activity to result in strong downburst winds. The first thunderstorm activity to develop across portions of southeast AZ resulted in the development of a strong outflow boundary, responsible for dense blowing dust conditions across Pinal and southern Maricopa Counties as well as sparking additional storms that affected portions of the Phoenix metro area. With the thunderstorms that moved across the Phoenix metro area, there were several reports of tree damage as well as structural damage to several homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree down in the area of 7th Street and Bell Road in Phoenix. Diameter of tree estimated to be about a foot. Wind gusts were estimated at 55-60 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first in a series of disturbances moved across Arizona from March 20 to 21. This caused widespread snow showers to develop.  The heaviest snow was over the higher terrain in upslope areas. This storm also delivered strong and damaging winds. The higher snow levels caused significant melting of lower elevation snow and flooding. The flooding took several days to flow down major rivers in northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Colorado River in Apache County began to flood early March 22. The river remained high until mid-day March 24.

CZ_NAME: UPPER GILA RIVER AND ARAVAIPA VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures 3 to 6 degrees above normal were experienced in the lower elevations of Pima, Santa Cruz, Graham, Greenlee and southeast Pinal counties June 24-30, with very hot temperatures 5 to 11 degrees above normal high temperatures breaking records in a few locations June 25-27. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was the primary cause of death for two individuals found in the desert, one in Santa Cruz County and another on the Tohono O'odham Nation. Heat was determined to be a contributing factor in the death of five individuals in Tucson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures 6 to 11 degrees above normal were experienced in the lower elevations of Cochise, Graham, Greenlee, Santa Cruz and southeast Pinal counties June 26-27, causing the Major Heat Risk category to be reached. Some of the hottest temperatures during the period were 110 degrees at Cascabel, Safford Airport (record for June 26), and Sasabe (records both days); 109 at Duncan; 108 at Nogales and Picacho; 107 at Bisbee-Douglas Airport; and 106 at San Manuel and Willcox.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures 3 to 6 degrees above normal were experienced in the lower elevations of Pima, Santa Cruz, Graham, Greenlee and southeast Pinal counties June 24-30, with very hot temperatures 5 to 11 degrees above normal high temperatures breaking records in a few locations June 25-27. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures 5 to 10 degrees above normal were experienced in the Tucson Metro area June 25-27, causing the Major Heat Risk category to be reached. The Tucson International Airport reached 111 degrees on June 25-26 and 108 on June 27. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that heat was a contributing factor in the death of one individual in Tucson found on the street on June 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure moving eastward across California brought a plume of deep moisture to Arizona as well. The synoptic-scale ascent from the trough combined with the moisture allowed for periods of heavy rain over Arizona March 15 and 16.  Surface dewpoints were similar to summer time...in the 40s and 50s. The heavy rainfall, high snow levels, and deep snow pack combined to produce rapid snow melt and significant runoff into area waterways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wet Beaver Creek gauge near Rimrock rose from 5.5 feet at 1000 AM MST on March 15 to 9.4 feet at 400 PM. The creek dropped back to 5.5 feet by noon on March 17. A local newspaper reported that areas along Wet Beaver Creek were evacuated.  This included Rimrock near Lake Montezuma and people along Beaver Vista Drive and Rimrock Drive.  An overnight shelter was set up at Camp Verde Middle School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm impacted northern and central Arizona March 1 and 2. The main trough moved southward along the West Coast to southern California then moved across central Arizona. This track gave northern Arizona substantial snow and lower elevation rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Black Mesa Chapter House received 12 inches of snow.  Inscription Chapter House received 10 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm impacted northern and central Arizona March 1 and 2. The main trough moved southward along the West Coast to southern California then moved across central Arizona. This track gave northern Arizona substantial snow and lower elevation rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mountains of Yavapai County (above 5,000 feet) received heavy snow. The Prescott area reported 8 to 15 inches of snow. The Iron Springs area received 11 inches of snow. Eight inches of snow fell 7 miles W-SW of Chino Valley at 5,100 feet. Nine inches of snow fell 10 miles west of Ash Fork. Ten inches of snow fell in Seligman.  Just over 11 inches (11.4 inches) was reported W-SW of Cherry at 5,200 feet. Ponderosa Park (3 miles east) reported 13 inches of new snow. Mingus West received 8 inches of snow. A foot of new snow fell 10 miles south of Ash Fort at an elevation of 5,280 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first in a series of disturbances moved across Arizona from March 20 to 21. This caused widespread snow showers to develop.  The heaviest snow was over the higher terrain in upslope areas. This storm also delivered strong and damaging winds. The higher snow levels caused significant melting of lower elevation snow and flooding. The flooding took several days to flow down major rivers in northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A stream gauge on the Agua Fria River near Black Canyon City reported a rise of over 15.5 feet. The river started to rise at around 800 AM on March 21. The river reached the flood stage around 400 AM March 22 and remained above flood stage until around 500 PM. The Rivers Edge RV Park was evacuated due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 22, three fires were started along State Route 92 between Sierra Vista and Palominas by a vehicle dragging a loose chain. The fires spread rapidly due to gusty winds with two fires combining to form the Wildhorse Fire. State Route 92 was closed for a time and residents of Three Canyons development were temporarily evacuated. While burning in a residential area, no houses were damaged, although four small outbuildings were destroyed. The fire consumed a total of 747 acres before becoming fully contained June 24. Fire suppression costs tallied $350,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On June 22, three fires were started along State Route 92 between Sierra Vista and Palominas by a vehicle dragging a loose chain. The fires spread rapidly due to gusty winds with two fires combining to form the Wildhorse Fire. State Route 92 was closed for a time and residents of Three Canyons development were temporarily evacuated. While burning in a residential area, no houses were damaged, although four small outbuildings were destroyed. The fire consumed a total of 747 acres before becoming fully contained June 24. Fire suppression costs tallied $350,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Anomalous snowfall across the high terrain of Arizona as well as lower elevation rainfall led to greater than normal runoff into the Salt and Verde watersheds. As a result of this, dam releases were conducted at multiple locations in these watersheds, leading to heightened flows in these rivers. Upstream releases led to additional releases from Granite Reef Dam, which initially began to cause impacts on the 4th when releases from the dam increased above 1,000 cfs. Granite Reef Dam releases continued to increase going through the month of March and at one point during the month was releasing nearly 40,000 cfs. Releases from this dam led to downstream flow in the normally dry Salt River, which runs through the Phoenix metro. The anomalous runoff into the Salt and Verde watersheds and the resultant dam releases led to numerous impacts along the Salt, Verde, and Gila Rivers, including multiple unbridged river crossings being flooded, multiple water rescues, and one recreational fatality. Flow in the Salt River continued downstream into the Gila River, causing more impacts along the way down to Painted Rock Dam. Releases continued through the remainder of the month and into April, resulting in prolonged impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two people kayaking on the Salt River approaching the Phon D Sutton Recreation Area became separated before one of them capsized on the 19th. The capsized kayaker was able to hold onto trees while the Maricopa County Sheriff's Office worked to rescue him via helicopter. The man was safely rescued with no injuries reported. Ongoing dam releases were resulting in strong currents in the river at the time. Event timing is based on downstream Granite Reef Dam releases.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first in a series of disturbances moved across Arizona from March 20 to 21. This caused widespread snow showers to develop.  The heaviest snow was over the higher terrain in upslope areas. This storm also delivered strong and damaging winds. The higher snow levels caused significant melting of lower elevation snow and flooding. The flooding took several days to flow down major rivers in northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tonto Creek near Roosevelt started to rise from a level of 3.7 feet (630 CFS) at around 1100 AM on March 21.  The flooding started earlier upstream. The first peak flow of 12.2 feet (23,300 CFS) was at 800 PM on March 21. A second peak of 12.5 feet (25,500 CFS) was observed at 800 AM on March 22. The gauge dropped back down to action level at (4.35 feet) on March 25 at 800 AM. An elderly couple and their dog were killed in the Bear Flat area when they cross the flooded Tonto Creek in their truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong ridge of high pressure strengthened over the Desert Southwest during the middle part of the month, resulting in well above normal temperatures and excessive heat conditions. Afternoon high temperatures in Phoenix during this time period ranged between 111-119 degrees, tying or breaking daily record highs on the 13th through the 15th as well as the 17th through the 20th. Record warm low temperatures were also set on the 12th through the 14th and the 16th through the 22nd. The warmest low temperature occurred on the 19th when Phoenix Sky Harbor only got down to 97 degrees, breaking the previous all-time record warm low of 96 degrees set in 2003. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 232 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this heat wave. One of the fatalities that occurred during this time include an elderly man who succumbed to the heat on the 16th in the Sun City Festival area after experiencing a flat tire during his bike ride. During the middle of July, morgue capacity quickly went from 64% to 94% due to the heat, resulting in temporary morgue operations being set up. Maricopa County daily heat related emergency department visits peaked on July 19th at 147. In addition to the heat fatalities that occurred in Maricopa County, seven heat-associated fatalities were also reported in La Paz County during this time.

A strong ridge of high pressure situated over the Desert Southwest led to excessive heat conditions across the region to start off July. High temperatures in Phoenix during this time ranged between 111-116 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, there were 10 heat-associated fatalities that occurred during this time. Additionally, one heat-associated fatality also occurred in La Paz County. The month of July would continue on to become the hottest month on record in Phoenix with high temperatures in Phoenix topping out at or above 110 degrees every day except for the 31st. Numerous heat records would be broken and the summer would become the deadliest on record for Phoenix with a total of 645 heat-associated deaths. In addition to the heat fatalities, Phoenix Fire Department responded to a total of 2,240 calls for heat-related illness, which is 36% higher than 2022.

A strong ridge of high pressure over the Southern Plains expanded westward into the Desert Southwest, leading to excessive heat conditions across the region. Record high temperatures were set on the 5th (116 degrees) and the 6th (114 degrees) in Phoenix, while record warm low temperatures were set on the 2nd (93 degrees), 3rd (92 degrees), and 7th (91 degrees). According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, there were a total of 55 heat-associated fatalities during this heat event. One of the heat fatalities that occurred during this time include a 34-year-old woman who went hiking at Deem Hills Recreation Area during the morning of the 4th but was later found deceased in a remote area near a trail. Additionally, two heat-associated fatalities occurred in La Paz County during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four heat-associated fatalities (2 direct and 2 indirect) were reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

Afternoon high temperatures observed at Phoenix Sky Harbor climbed between 111-115 degrees during the August 15-17th timeframe. On the 16th, the high temperature of 115 degrees tied the record high for the date set back in 2020. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, there were 20 heat-associated fatalities that occurred during this event. Additionally, one heat-associated fatality occurred in La Paz County during this time.

Strong persistent high pressure centered over the Desert Southwest led to dangerous excessive heat conditions across the region during the middle part of July. Anomalous, record-breaking heat was observed during the month, providing little relief. On the 16th, an elderly man went on a bike ride around noon in the Sun City Festival area when he then experienced a flat tire about an hour into his ride. After failing to meet his wife at their agreed upon meeting location, she called 911. First responders located the man deceased less than a mile from the meeting location. The Maricopa County Office of the Medical Examiner determined that the cause of death was due to the heat. Temperatures at the time this occurred were around 107-110 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 73-year-old man went on a bike ride in the Sun City Festival area around 1130MST on the 16th before experiencing a flat tire about an hour into his ride. He then called his wife and agreed to walk and meet her at a nearby fire department on Wagner Complex Drive so she could pick him up. His wife then called 911 after he didn't show up. Police and Buckeye Fire Department searched for the man and found his body in the desert less than a mile from the meeting location where he was planning to meet his wife. The Maricopa County Office of the Medical Examiner determined that his death was due to the heat. Temperatures during the early afternoon hours in this area were around 107-110 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong ridge of high pressure strengthened over the Desert Southwest during the middle part of the month, resulting in well above normal temperatures and excessive heat conditions. Afternoon high temperatures in Phoenix during this time period ranged between 111-119 degrees, tying or breaking daily record highs on the 13th through the 15th as well as the 17th through the 20th. Record warm low temperatures were also set on the 12th through the 14th and the 16th through the 22nd. The warmest low temperature occurred on the 19th when Phoenix Sky Harbor only got down to 97 degrees, breaking the previous all-time record warm low of 96 degrees set in 2003. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 232 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this heat wave. One of the fatalities that occurred during this time include an elderly man who succumbed to the heat on the 16th in the Sun City Festival area after experiencing a flat tire during his bike ride. During the middle of July, morgue capacity quickly went from 64% to 94% due to the heat, resulting in temporary morgue operations being set up. Maricopa County daily heat related emergency department visits peaked on July 19th at 147. In addition to the heat fatalities that occurred in Maricopa County, seven heat-associated fatalities were also reported in La Paz County during this time.

A strong ridge of high pressure situated over the Desert Southwest led to excessive heat conditions across the region to start off July. High temperatures in Phoenix during this time ranged between 111-116 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, there were 10 heat-associated fatalities that occurred during this time. Additionally, one heat-associated fatality also occurred in La Paz County. The month of July would continue on to become the hottest month on record in Phoenix with high temperatures in Phoenix topping out at or above 110 degrees every day except for the 31st. Numerous heat records would be broken and the summer would become the deadliest on record for Phoenix with a total of 645 heat-associated deaths. In addition to the heat fatalities, Phoenix Fire Department responded to a total of 2,240 calls for heat-related illness, which is 36% higher than 2022.

Monsoon moisture moved into far northern Arizona and triggered a strong thunderstorm over the Arizona portion of Lake Powell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in Antelope Canyon. It was closed for a day to remove the flood debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture moved into far northern Arizona and triggered a strong thunderstorm over the Arizona portion of Lake Powell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm wind gusts observed in Face Canyon (Lake Powell) with considerable blowing sand.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant monsoon moisture triggered thunderstorms with heavy rain and flash flooding over central Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fossil Creek Road (Forest Road 708) closed for several days due to damage from heavy             rain and flooding that occurred on August 16th and 17th. Closure began morning of             August 18th and remain closed for several week or more.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture and the  remains of tropical storm Harold brought thunderstorms a local heavy rain to parts of northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Schultz (2010) and Pipeline (2022) fire scars brought debris flowing over Copland Lane. A road grader was needed to clear the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture and the  remains of tropical storm Harold brought thunderstorms a local heavy rain to parts of northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms moving south over the Paria River basin (including in Utah) caused flash flooding. The river gauge on the Paria River near the Colorado River (Lees Ferry) measured a rise in water the level 4.8 feet at 8:15 PM MST to 6.6 feet at 11:00 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The air flow over Arizona slowly transitioned from a west to east trajectory, to more southerly. This was due to a nearly stationary low forming off the western coast of California, which will provided some dynamical forcing. This allowed moisture to move northward from Mexico. Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms formed over the north-central Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Schultz Fire (2010) scar and the Pipeline/Tunnel Fire (2022) caused the Upper Campbell drainage to flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The air flow over Arizona slowly transitioned from a west to east trajectory, to more southerly. This was due to a nearly stationary low forming off the western coast of California, which will provided some dynamical forcing. This allowed moisture to move northward from Mexico. Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms formed over the north-central Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A USGS stream gage on the Little Colorado River at Woodruff showed a rise of 6.9 feet starting at 120 PM on August 9. The flow returned to a normal level around 1000 PM on August 10. The flood started at Hoolbrook at 600 PM August and rose around 4.5 feet by 200 AM on August 10. This flood reached the Colorado River at 500 PM on August 10 and the river level rose 1.7 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The air flow over Arizona slowly transitioned from a west to east trajectory, to more southerly. This was due to a nearly stationary low forming off the western coast of California, which will provided some dynamical forcing. This allowed moisture to move northward from Mexico. Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms formed over the north-central Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Landspout tornado was  spotted 10 miles north of Cameron, Arizona at 1:50 MDT. Only visible lofted debris was dust. Dissipated within 10 minutes after sighting. Video on Twitter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The air flow over Arizona slowly transitioned from a west to east trajectory, to more southerly. This was due to a nearly stationary low forming off the western coast of California, which will provided some dynamical forcing. This allowed moisture to move northward from Mexico. Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms formed over the north-central Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread standing water was seen next to State Route 77. The highway was not flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture over northern Arizona triggered thunderstorms that caused flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Indian Route 15 was closed due to flooding at mile post 95 between Cornfields and Greasewood. Report was from Navajo Nation Road Conditions Facebook page.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge over the central United States and a trough draped across the West Coast put Arizona in a position to receive a moist southerly wind flow. The 12Z sounding from|measured 0.86 inches of Precipitable Water which was slightly above average for late August...adequate monsoonal moisture to fuel storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 1 to 2.5 inches of rain fell in around two hours just east and northeast of the town of Tusayan and the South Entrance to The Grand Canyon National Park. This caused significant flooding of homes homes, buildings, and cars. Highway 64 into the GCNP was closed, shelter in place orders were put into place, and children were stuck in their school             because they could not safely get home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough moving into the Pacific Coast and a high pressure center east of Arizona brought a strong and moist southerly flow over Arizona. This brought scattered showers and thunderstorms over the high terrain with local heavy rain and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to 1.5 inch diameter hail was reported in the Payson area between 330 and 345 PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture continued to move northward over northern Arizona with more thunderstorms developing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sedona Airport recorded a 60 MPH peak wind gust from a thunderstorm outflow at 315 PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture increased again over northern Arizona which allowed thunderstorms to develop over parts of northern Arizona. Strong winds hit the Prescott area in the afternoon and there was flash flooding in southern Yavapai County late in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station two miles north of the Prescott Courthouse reported a peak wind gust of 64 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture increased again over northern Arizona which allowed thunderstorms to develop over parts of northern Arizona. Strong winds hit the Prescott area in the afternoon and there was flash flooding in southern Yavapai County late in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Agua Fria River near Cordes Junction rose 2.2 feet between 1100 PM on July 22 and midnight on July 23. The water remained elevated for about 12 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture increased again over northern Arizona which allowed thunderstorms to develop over parts of northern Arizona. Strong winds hit the Prescott area in the afternoon and there was flash flooding in southern Yavapai County late in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Black Canyon Creek near Cleator rose to 2.92 feet (1026 CFS).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture moving north across northern Arizona and southern Utah brought heavy rain from thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over Agassiz Peak and the area between Agassiz Peak and Fremont Peak caused a flash flood on the southern side of Agassiz Peak. The Kachina Trail had two deep channels cut through it about 400 yards apart. Those channels were 10 to 12 feet deep and extended above and below the trail. Some of the rocks in the flow were 3 to 5 feet in diameter. The two channels joined and the debris, water, and mud flowed southwestward. Most of the debris formed an alluvial fan just uphill from Freidlein Prairie Road. Water and mud did flow across that road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture continued to move across northern Arizona with strong to severe  thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An automated stream gauge on Cataract Creek (9 miles SSE of the Village of Supai) measured a 6 foot rise at 315 PM. A gauge near the confluence with the Colorado River showed a 1.6 foot rise starting at around 400 AM MST on July 31.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture moving north across northern Arizona and southern Utah brought heavy rain from thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over Agassiz Peak caused a flash flood on the northeastern side of the peak Peak into Locket Meadow. The water catchment in Snowslide Canyon was plugged with debris and damaged. One of the pump houses in the Inner Basin was damaged. The waterline that provides up to 30 percent of the water in Flagstaff during the summer was exposed and damaged in several locations. The access road around the water houses on Waterline Road was washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first in a series of low pressure systems brought locally heavy snow to the mountains of far northeast Arizona November 29 to November 30.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of snow fell around Del Muerto (8 miles E-NE of Chinle) at 6,400 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong area of high pressure that brought record heat to northern Arizona in the previous few days started to move eastward. This allowed a southerly flow to develop over the area transporting moisture northward from Mexico. This moisture allowed for the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms over parts of northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds broke large tree limbs, uprooted trees, blew shingles off structures, and caused power outages for much of the city of Page.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture moving north across northern Arizona and southern Utah brought heavy rain from thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across Coconino County. Some of the 24 hour rainfall amounts include (in inches): Arizona Snowbowl: 2.92, Bright Angel Point North Rim: 1.14, Four Springs RAWS: 1.13, Mable Canyon: 0.74, Tusayan: 0.73, and Jacob Lake 0.71.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering low pressure and monsoon moisture fueled scattered thunderstorms over northwest Arizona. A few produced flash flooding and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and rocks flowed over Highway 66 near mile marker 91.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering low pressure and monsoon moisture fueled scattered thunderstorms over northwest Arizona. A few produced flash flooding and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A web cam showed dust storm conditions that reduced visibility to near zero 15 miles NNE of Dolan Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fresh push of monsoon moisture fueled another round of thunderstorms over northwest Arizona. Several storms produced flash flooding and damaging winds.
A fresh push of monsoon moisture fueled another round of thunderstorms over northwest Arizona. Several storms produced flash flooding and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Oatman-Topock Highway was closed from Tule Dr to I-40 due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fresh push of monsoon moisture fueled another round of thunderstorms over northwest Arizona. Several storms produced flash flooding and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds in northern Kingman destroyed an aluminum canopy and blew down multiple power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fresh push of monsoon moisture fueled another round of thunderstorms over northwest Arizona. Several storms produced flash flooding and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding closed North Estrella Rd from Chino Dr to West Estrella Circle and Agua Fria Dr from Milky Way Rd to North Estrella Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fresh push of monsoon moisture fueled another round of thunderstorms over northwest Arizona. Several storms produced flash flooding and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding in Dolan Springs closed 7th St from Sandra Dr to Mineral Rd and 1st St from Agua Mawsa Dr to Sandra Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fresh push of monsoon moisture fueled another round of thunderstorms over northwest Arizona. Several storms produced flash flooding and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 66 was flooded 4 miles ENE of New Kingman-Butler.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: West Cottonwood Rd was closed between mile markers 2 and 16 due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lingering monsoon moisture and weakly unstable air fueled scattered thunderstorms over northwest Arizona. One produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rapid onset flooding of Short Creek closed Hildale St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, elongated upper level ridge situated across the Desert Southwest and the Southern Plains, leading to well above normal temperatures and excessive heat conditions. The official temperature in Phoenix peaked at 115 degrees during the afternoon on the 4th. According to local media reports, a 34-year-old woman went hiking at the Deem Hills Recreation Area during the morning of the 4th. She was later reported missing around midday after failing to return from her hike after several hours. Her body was discovered in a remote area near a trail during the early evening hours. The Maricopa County Office of the Medical Examiner determined that heat was a contributing factor in the hiker's death.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 34-year-old woman went hiking at the Deem Hills Recreation Area around 0830MST when temperatures were in the low 90s, according to a nearby observation. She was then reported missing around noon after failing to return from her hike. Midday temperatures in the area were around 100-105 degrees. Family and friends searched for the missing hiker with firefighters and police. Her body was later discovered around 1700MST in a remote area near a trail. According to the Maricopa County Office of the Medical Examiner, heat was a contributing factor in the hiker's death.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure centered over eastern Arizona and New Mexico brought seasonal moisture into eastern sections of Arizona. Some showers and thunderstorms developed that produced locally heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gauge on White River (WHTA3) near Ft Apache showed a 400 CFS spike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep seasonal moisture move northward over northern Arizona with numerous showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning from a Monsoon thunderstorm destroyed a house in Baderville, north of Flagstaff Monday. Summit Fire and Medical crews arrived at the home just after 12:30 p.m. on a report of the roof of the home on fire. As crews got closer to the scene, firefighters could see smoke from Highway 180 and the fire was moved to a first alarm. Initial crews arrived to find heavy smoke coming from the attic of the home. More crews arrived to work the fire, including two water tenders. In all a total of 11 units and two battalion chiefs responded to the fire. There were no injuries to civilians or fire personnel and the home is a total loss (KAFF Country News).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong area of high pressure that brought record heat to northern Arizona in the previous few days started to move eastward. This allowed a southerly flow to develop over the area transporting moisture northward from Mexico. This moisture allowed for the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms over parts of northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A short lived-landspout tornado video was relayed to the NWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture continued to move northward over northern Arizona with more thunderstorms developing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Verde River near Clarkdale rose from 1.59 feet to 3.84 feet between 900 PM and 1000 PM. The river remained elevated until the next afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high pressure center located over New Mexico brought deep moisture over northern Arizona. In addition, an inverted trough moving northward in this flow brought additional instability which triggered stronger storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported in the Yavapai Hills area. One inch diameter hail was reported near Costco about 30 minutes later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture continued to move across northern Arizona with strong to severe  thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Schultz (2010) and Pipeline (2022) fire scars lead to flooding on the west end of Copeland Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough over the Great Basin gradually moved eastward through Utah into Colorado. As this occurred, a shortwave dropped through southern Utah/northern Arizona late on November 24 into November 25. Southerly flow and increasing pressure gradient,  increased moisture advection into the region. Showers began to develop over southern Utah and southern Nevada during the morning of November 24. The cold front was quite dynamic as it traveled across Arizona with thunderstorms north of Flagstaff with rather decent rainfall rates. The Kaibab Plateau and Chuska Mountains received the greatest amounts of precipitation before the storm moved east on November 25.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow fell at North Rim Entrance Station of the Grand Canyon National Park at 8,800 feet elevation. Four inches of snow fell at the Bright Angel Ranger Station on the North Rim (edge) of the Grand Canyon at just over 8,200 feet elevation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first in a series of low pressure systems brought locally heavy snow to the mountains of far northeast Arizona November 29 to November 30.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of snow fell around Del Muerto (8 miles E-NE of Chinle) at 6,400 feet.  Six inches of snow fell in Chinle at 5,500 feet. Six inches of snow also fell in Many Farms at 5,300 feet elevation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The extreme (D3) drought category continued across far southern and eastern sections of southeast Arizona during November. The area near the New Mexico line encompassed southern Greenlee, southeast Graham and eastern Cochise counties, including the Chiricahua Mountains; while areas near the International Border stretched from the Huachuca Mountains westward across Santa Cruz County to the Buenos Aires National Wildlife Refuge in southeast Pima County. While rain fell on several days in November, monthly totals were less than a quarter inch, less than 25 percent of normal.  Soil moisture readings remained below the 5th or 10th percentile ranking in most areas through November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first in a series of disturbances moved across Arizona from March 20 to 21. This caused widespread snow showers to develop.  The heaviest snow was over the higher terrain in upslope areas. This storm also delivered strong and damaging winds. The higher snow levels caused significant melting of lower elevation snow and flooding. The flooding took several days to flow down major rivers in northern Arizona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stream gauge on Oak Creek in Sedona rose above action stage at  (7 feet) at 900 AM on March 21.  It reached a peak of 16.35 feet at 600 AM on March 22 and then dropped below 7 feet around 1100 PM March 22.  Flood stage is at 14.0 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures 7 to 15 degrees above normal caused the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached in parts of southeast Arizona. The Pima County Medical Examiner determined heat to be the primary cause of death for three individuals found in the desert: two in Santa Cruz County and a third in far southwest Pima County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified ridge of high pressure advanced eastward across the western United States during the middle part of the month, causing temperatures to climb above normal across the Phoenix area. The warmest day during this time period occurred on the 16th when Phoenix Sky Harbor recorded a high temperature of 107 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 5 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two direct fatalities were reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, there were 12 heat-associated fatalities that occurred during this event.

The area near the New Mexico line encompassed southern Greenlee, southeast Graham and eastern Cochise counties, including the Chiricahua Mountains; while areas near the International Border stretched from the Huachuca Mountains westward across Santa Cruz County to the Buenos Aires National Wildlife Refuge in southeast Pima County. Many locations within the extreme drought area only received 20-40 percent of normal rainfall during the monsoon. Soil moisture readings fell below the 5th percentile ranking by the end of September in most areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge of high pressure strengthened over the Desert Southwest during the time period between the 6-11th, leading to excessive heat conditions across parts of the region. In Phoenix, record high temperatures were set on the 8th, 9th, and 10th. The hottest temperature recorded during this time occurred on the the 9th when Phoenix Sky Harbor recorded a high of 114 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, there were 12 heat-associated fatalities that occurred during this event.

Very hot temperatures were observed across southeast Arizona September 9 and 10. High temperatures were 10 to 14 degrees above normal causing the Major Heat Risk categories to be reached. A total of four heat-related fatalities were reported by the Pima County Medical Examiner.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures around 10 degrees above normal caused the Major Heat Risk category to be reached across the Tucson Metro area September 9-10. Record highs at Tucson International Airport were 108 and 111 degrees, respectively. The Pima County Medical Examiner determined that heat was a contributing factor in the death an elderly individual in their Tucson back yard. Heart disease was a complicating factor in this fatality.

While the heaviest rainfall and flash flooding remained to the west across southern California, Tropical Storm Hilary did bring very strong winds to parts of southwest Arizona, which also led to areas of dense blowing dust. Hilary brought strong low level winds upwards of 50-60 knots at 850 mb and 925 mb, which mixed to the surface at times. As a result, very strong winds at the surface led to instances of damage along with low visibility due to blowing dust across Yuma County. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 69 mph gust was measured at KNYL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures 10 to 12 degrees above normal caused the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached. The Pima County Medical Examiner determined heat to be the primary cause of death for two individuals found in the desert on the Tohono O'odham Nation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures 10 to 12 degrees above normal caused the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached on the Tohono O'odham Nation where temperatures topped out around 102 degrees. The Pima County Medical Examiner determined heat to be the primary cause of death for two individuals found in the desert southwest in the Sells and San Miguel areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep southerly fetch of rich moisture up from the Gulf of California led to scattered to widespread heavy rainfall in portions of Southwest Arizona during the afternoon of the 1st. Moisture values were well above normal for the time of year with precipitable water values analyzed around 2.0-2.2 inches. Favorable dynamic and orographic forcing helped lead to strong updrafts and training of storms over certain terrain areas. Radar estimated rain rates were up to around 2-3 inches per hour with locally higher rates over shorter periods. These high rain rates led to areas of high rainfall accumulation, with some areas of more than 1 inch of rain and isolated areas up to 2.5-3.5 inches, radar estimated. Highest measured rainfall were between 1.5-2.0 inches. This resulted in multiple reports of flash flooding in the region, including flooding of the Gila River in Yuma County. On top of the flooding, the heavy mass loading, strong updrafts, and strong cold pools resulted in strong to severe outflow winds emanating out from the showers and storms. , which led to a few severe wind reports and generated dust storm conditions in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Yuma Proving Ground Station located about 15 miles north of I-8 between Wellton and Tacna measured a 62 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low pressure system just off the California Coast and the subtropical high pressure system over the southern Plains led to strong southerly flow across Arizona. This helped transport higher moisture content northward with precipitable water values reaching above 1.5. This led to moderate instability across southern Arizona with MLCAPE values of approximately 1000 J/KG. A strong thunderstorm complex developed near the international border across western Pima County. This thunderstorm complex sent a robust outflow northward into southwest Maricopa County leading to dense blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility down to as low as a few hundred feet was reported along I-8 west of Sentinel due to dense blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southeasterly flow aloft promoted moisture advection (PWATs 1.5-1.7) into south-central Arizona along with the needed steering flow to allow thunderstorms across southeast Arizona to progress towards the Phoenix area. Effective bulk wind shear values around 35-40 knots allowed for the development of organized thunderstorms, becoming strong to severe across central and southeast Arizona. MLCAPE during this event was around 500-1,000 J/kg while DCAPE around 1,000 J/kg promoted damaging winds. During the early evening hours on the 21st, a severe thunderstorm produced damaging winds that knocked down multiple trees in Apache Junction. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mobile home at Lost Dutchman RV Resort had most of its roof peeled back due to damaging winds. No injuries were reported.

A slow moving winter storm delivered heavy snow to the mountains of southeast Arizona January 15 through 17. Snow amounts in the high terrain above 7000 ft totaled 1 to 2 feet. Rain amounts totaled 1 to 3 inches below 5000 feet with some locations in eastern Pima County receiving more than 4 inches of rain. The resultant runoff filled larger stream channels leading to rescues of several individuals caught in the flowing water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mount Graham observatory reported 12 to 24 inches of snow on the summit of Mount Graham.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SANTA CRUZ RIVER AND ALTAR VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent strong high pressure aloft created an extended period of very hot temperatures in early July across the lower elevations of southeast Arizona. High temperatures were 5 to 14 degrees above normal causing the Major Heat Risk category to be reached and several record high temperatures to be established. A total of 13 heat-related fatalities were reported by the Pima County Medical Examiner.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures of 7 to 10 degrees above normal caused the Major Heat Risk category to be reached the first eight days of July. Record highs of 106 degrees on July 6 and 105 on July 3 and 7 occurred at the Nogales Airport and 106 degrees on July 3 at Sasabe. The hottest temperature was 107 at Sasabe on July 4 and 5. The Pima County Medical Examiner determined that heat was the primary cause of death for one individual found in the desert near Arivaca.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed and moved west across southeast Arizona during the afternoon and evening. Severe thunderstorm outflow gusts occurred in Graham and Greenlee counties and some tree damage occurred in Tucson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust to 61 mph was measured at KSAD ASOS at 1634MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved developed and moved west across Graham, Pinal and Pima counties during the late afternoon and evening. The Tucson Metro was hit hard by large hail and damaging winds. Numerous power poles were downed resulting in the loss of power for more than 50,000 customers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranging from the size of quarters to golf balls fell from Speedway and Wilmot to Broadway and Campbell. There were sporadic reports of damage to car windows and skylights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two lightning-caused fires began on Black Mountain, 18 miles north of Safford, on July 29. Winds and complex terrain caused the fires to merge on July 30 to form the Treadway Fire. A total of 8400 acres were consumed before extreme fire behavior was tempered by firefighting efforts and rainfall on August 3. Fire suppression costs tallied nearly $1.4 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two lightning-caused fires began on Black Mountain, 18 miles north of Safford, on July 29. Winds and complex terrain caused the fires to merge on July 30 to form the Treadway Fire. A total of 8400 acres were consumed before extreme fire behavior was tempered by firefighting efforts and rainfall on August 3. Fire suppression costs tallied nearly $1.4 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed and moved southwest across southeast Arizona during the afternoon. One storm produced a severe wind gust in Greenlee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 65 mph was measured at the Trail Cabin RAWS. Time estimated from radar at 1315MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved through southeast Arizona resulting in heavy snow and strong winds across some of the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph combined with snowfall totals of 6 to 12 inches occurred over the White Mountains of Graham and Greenlee Counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved through southeast Arizona resulting in heavy snow and strong winds across some of the higher elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph combined with snowfall totals of 6 to 12 inches occurred over the Santa Catalina Mountains including Summerhaven and Mount Lemmon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent strong high pressure aloft created an extended period of very hot temperatures in early July across the lower elevations of southeast Arizona. High temperatures were 5 to 14 degrees above normal causing the Major Heat Risk category to be reached and several record high temperatures to be established. A total of 13 heat-related fatalities were reported by the Pima County Medical Examiner.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures of 5 to 9 degrees above normal caused the Major Heat Risk category to be reached the first eight days of July. High temperatures at the Tucson International Airport ranged from 107 to 110 degrees. While just shy of record levels, 110 was reached on July 2, 3, 6 and 7. The Pima County Medical Examiner determined that heat was the primary cause of death for one individual outside of a commercial building in Littletown. Heat was determined to be a contributing factor for 6 other individuals in Tucson. Three individuals who suffered from heart disease were found in their homes with A/C that had failed or was not on. Three others were found outdoors with complicating factors stemming from drug toxicity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent strong high pressure aloft created an extended period of very hot temperatures in early July across the lower elevations of southeast Arizona.
