A total of 13 heat-related fatalities were reported by the Pima County Medical Examiner.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures of 4 to 7 degrees above normal caused the Major Heat Risk category to be reached across southeast Pinal County July 2-8. High temperatures of 106 on July 6 and 105 on July 7 reached record levels at San Manuel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and moved northwest across southeast Arizona through the evening. Storms produced damaging winds and large hail across eastern Pima County and the Tohono O'odham Nation and a dust storm in Pinal County. The Catalina Foothills were especially hit hard with numerous power poles downed leaving thousands without power for two days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph was measured at KDMA ASOS at 1634MST. A spotter recorded a gust to 68 mph near Speedway and Harrison at 1635MST and a gust to 63 mph was measured at KTUS ASOS at 1638MST.  In the Catalina Foothills from River/Swan to Skyline/Campbell numerous trees were downed, some of which fell on parking structures, vehicles and apartment buildings.  Around 7500 customers were without power due to 30 power poles being downed. Winds peeled parts of roofs off a handful of houses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved developed and moved west across Graham, Pinal and Pima counties during the late afternoon and evening. The Tucson Metro was hit hard by large hail and damaging winds. Numerous power poles were downed resulting in the loss of power for more than 50,000 customers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused extensive damage from the northeast to the south side of Tucson, from near Sabino High School southwestward to the Tucson International Airport. At least 40 power poles were downed or damaged, dozens of large trees were downed or uprooted, and several homes had portions of roofs peeled off by the winds.  Apartment complexes near Broadway and Camino Seco and 22nd and Kolb were especially hard hit as falling trees damaged a number of units, car shelters, and vehicles. Just south of this area along Golf Links Rd between Kolb and Wilmot and on Davis-Monthan AFB, numerous power poles were downed or damaged. Around 50,000 customers lost power as a result of the storms. A wind gusts to 62 mph was recorded at KDMA ASOS at 1818MST and 64 mph at KTUS ASOS at 1825MST.

Sporadic, mainly minor, wind damage  also occurred near Valencia and Camino Verde.

Cold temperatures settled into southeast Arizona on the morning of February 16 after the passage of a winter storm system. While temperatures warmed up the following night, strong winds created cold wind chill conditions into February 17. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that the primary cause of death was hypothermia for two individuals found in the Santa Rita Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures fell into the teens during the morning of February 16 with wind chills in the single digits. While air temperatures were not as cold for the next 24 hours, winds increased to 20 to 30 mph with gusts of 50 to 60 mph and created wind chills as cold as the teens into the morning of February 17. The Pima County Medical Examiner confirmed that the primary cause of death was hypothermia for two individuals found in the Santa Rita Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously intense low pressure system for February caused very strong winds across southeast Arizona resulting in damage such as roof damage, downed trees, power poles and communication antennas. The Tucson International Airport recorded its strongest February wind gust (51 MPH) in at least the past 50 years. Elsewhere, especially across Cochise, Santa Cruz, Graham and Greenlee Counties, wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were common in valleys with gusts higher than 70 mph at higher elevations. A 92 mph gust was recorded at the Guthrie RAWS. The combination of the strong winds and snow (6 to 12 in the mountains) resulted in blizzard-like conditions at times above 7000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were common across the White Mountains along with a storm total of 6 to 12 inches of snow with blizzard-like conditions at times. Portions of Highway 191 were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry southwesterly flow aloft with a strong ridge of high pressure to the east over the Southern Plains led to temperatures climbing near or in excess of 110 degrees in Phoenix at the end of June. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, seven heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure continued to create hot temperatures 3 to 5 degrees above normal  July 9-13 in parts of southeast Arizona and caused the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached.  A total of 18 heat-related fatalities were reported by the Pima County Medical Examiner.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures up to 6 degrees above normal caused the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached in eastern Pima County where temperatures topped out between 101 and 107 degrees.  A total of 14 heat-related fatalities were reported by the Pima County Medical Examiner. Heat was determined to be the primary cause of death for two individuals found in the desert, one near Three Points on the 12th and another in the Ironwood Forest National Monument on the 13th. Heat was also found to the primary cause of death for four individuals in Tucson. Two individuals were found on the 13th on Tucson streets and subsequently died at the hospital. Another was found deceased outdoors on the 13th and a fourth died on the 12th in their home where the A/C had been turned off. Heat was found to be a contributing factor in the deaths of 8 individuals in Tucson. Two persons died in their homes in which the A/C unit was either broken or turned off. They both also had complicating factors of heart disease. Six others were found outside at various locations in the city. The complicating factor for four of them was drug toxicity, while the other two suffered from other health issues.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge of high pressure strengthened over the Desert Southwest during the time period between the 6-11th, leading to excessive heat conditions across parts of the region. In Phoenix, record high temperatures were set on the 8th, 9th, and 10th. The hottest temperature recorded during this time occurred on the the 9th when Phoenix Sky Harbor recorded a high of 114 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, there were 12 heat-associated fatalities that occurred during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving winter storm delivered heavy snow to the mountains of southeast Arizona January 15 through 17. Snow amounts in the high terrain above 7000 ft totaled 1 to 2 feet. The resultant runoff filled larger stream channels leading to rescues of several individuals caught in the flowing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The human caused Beehive Fire started during the evening of June 30 north of Ruby Road in Santa Cruz County. Due to hot and dry conditions in early July, the fire spread to 300 acres on July 1, to 3000 acres in the rugged terrain of Beehive Canyon by July 3 and topped 10,000 acres by July 8 as it entered extreme southeast Pima County. The fire consumed 10,745 acres before becoming fully contained on July 12. Fire suppression costs tallied nearly $5 million. The much smaller Beehive 2 Fire spotted just south of the Beehive Fire on July 8 and was aggressively suppressed, with costs tallying another $7 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously intense low pressure system for February caused very strong winds across southeast Arizona resulting in damage such as roof damage, downed trees, power poles and communication antennas. The Tucson International Airport recorded its strongest February wind gust (51 MPH) in at least the past 50 years. Elsewhere, especially across Cochise, Santa Cruz, Graham and Greenlee Counties, wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were common in valleys with gusts higher than 70 mph at higher elevations. A 92 mph gust was recorded at the Guthrie RAWS. The combination of the strong winds and snow (6 to 12 in the mountains) resulted in blizzard-like conditions at times above 7000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Guthrie RAWS at 6300 ft MSL recorded several gusts higher than 65 mph and a peak gust of 92 mph at 1203MST.  A portable RAWS at 4800 ft MSL near the Galiuro Mountains recorded a gust to 65 mph at 1128MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms moved northwest across parts of Graham, Cochise, Pinal and Pima counties during the afternoon and evening. Sporadic wind damage and isolated flash flooding occurred in the Tucson Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a large dead tree that fell onto a pickup while driving along Owens Pl.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms moved northwest across parts of Graham, Cochise, Pinal and Pima counties during the afternoon and evening. Sporadic wind damage and isolated flash flooding occurred in the Tucson Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a power pole and a tree at the Tucson International Airport. A thunderstorm wind gust to 58 mph was measured at KTUS ASOS at 1752MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms moved northwest across parts of Graham, Cochise, Pinal and Pima counties during the afternoon and evening. Sporadic wind damage and isolated flash flooding occurred in the Tucson Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust to 72 mph was recorded at KAVQ AWOS at 1935MST.

CZ_NAME: UPPER GILA RIVER AND ARAVAIPA VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two lightning-caused fires began on Black Mountain, 18 miles north of Safford, on July 29. Winds and complex terrain caused the fires to merge on July 30 to form the Treadway Fire. A total of 8400 acres were consumed before extreme fire behavior was tempered by firefighting efforts and rainfall on August 3. Fire suppression costs tallied nearly $1.4 million.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two lightning-caused fires began on Black Mountain, 18 miles north of Safford, on July 29. Winds and complex terrain caused the fires to merge on July 30 to form the Treadway Fire. A total of 8400 acres were consumed before extreme fire behavior was tempered by firefighting efforts and rainfall on August 3. Fire suppression costs tallied nearly $1.4 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed and moved southwest across southeast Arizona during the afternoon and evening. Large hail, severe wind gusts, and localized flash flooding occurred on the east side of Tucson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 1-1.5 in less than one hour caused Robb Wash to flow 2-3 feet deep across Pima Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed and moved northwest across southeast Arizona during the afternoon and evening. Wind damage took place in Whetstone and also at Saguaro National Park West where hundreds of saguaros were downed or damaged. Large hail occurred in south and central Tucson, damaging an elementary school. Moderate rain from a subsequent thunderstorm leaked into the building, causing ceiling tile damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew the corrugated metal roof off a machine shed in Whetstone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously intense low pressure system for February caused very strong winds across southeast Arizona resulting in damage such as roof damage, downed trees, power poles and communication antennas. The Tucson International Airport recorded its strongest February wind gust (51 MPH) in at least the past 50 years. Elsewhere, especially across Cochise, Santa Cruz, Graham and Greenlee Counties, wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were common in valleys with gusts higher than 70 mph at higher elevations. A 92 mph gust was recorded at the Guthrie RAWS. The combination of the strong winds and snow (6 to 12 in the mountains) resulted in blizzard-like conditions at times above 7000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Desert Research Institute station at 6800 ft MSL in the Chiricahua Mountains measured a gust to 65 mph at 1020MST and 66 mph at 1410MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge of high pressure strengthened over the Desert Southwest during the time period between the 6-11th, leading to excessive heat conditions across parts of the region. In Phoenix, record high temperatures were set on the 8th, 9th, and 10th. The hottest temperature recorded during this time occurred on the the 9th when Phoenix Sky Harbor recorded a high of 114 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, there were 12 heat-associated fatalities that occurred during this event.

The lightning caused Eskiminzin Fire started on July 23 and burned 1,113 acres on the southwest side of the San Carlos Apache Reservation northeast of Dudleyville before becoming contained on July 26. Fire suppression costs tallied $350,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The lightning caused Eskiminzin Fire started on July 23 and burned 1,113 acres on the southwest side of the San Carlos Apache Reservation northeast of Dudleyville before becoming contained on July 26. Fire suppression costs tallied $350,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure continued to create hot temperatures 3 to 5 degrees above normal  July 9-13 in parts of southeast Arizona and caused the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached.  A total of 18 heat-related fatalities were reported by the Pima County Medical Examiner.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures about 3 degrees above normal caused the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached in eastern Cochise County. Temperatures topped out at 102 degrees at San Simon and the Bisbee-Douglas Airport. The Pima County Medical Examiner determined heat to be a contributing factor in the death of one individual found in the desert 10 miles south of San Simon. A complicating factor in the fatality was drug toxicity.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SANTA CRUZ RIVER AND ALTAR VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure continued to create hot temperatures 3 to 5 degrees above normal  July 9-13 in parts of southeast Arizona and caused the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached.  A total of 18 heat-related fatalities were reported by the Pima County Medical Examiner.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures 3 to 5 degrees above normal and near 100 degrees caused the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached in the Altar Valley of Pima County and in Santa Cruz County on July 12. The Pima County Medical Examiner determined heat to be the primary cause of death for one individual found in the desert 5 miles east of Arivaca.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed and moved west across Graham and Cochise counties during the afternoon. One storm produced severe wind gusts at Safford.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Safford Airport ASOS KSAD recorded a wind gust of 60 mph at 1556MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced significant snow in the higher elevations of southeast Arizona. Rain and strong winds affected the valleys. A few thunderstorms also occurred across Pima and Pinal counties. One storm downed a tree causing significant damage to a residence in Marana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Mount Graham observatory, about 12 of snow occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced significant snow in the higher elevations of southeast Arizona. Rain and strong winds affected the valleys. A few thunderstorms also occurred across Pima and Pinal counties. One storm downed a tree causing significant damage to a residence in Marana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a shallow-rooted tree which fell onto a mobile home, causing significant roof and wall damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously intense low pressure system for February caused very strong winds across southeast Arizona resulting in damage such as roof damage, downed trees, power poles and communication antennas. The Tucson International Airport recorded its strongest February wind gust (51 MPH) in at least the past 50 years. Elsewhere, especially across Cochise, Santa Cruz, Graham and Greenlee Counties, wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were common in valleys with gusts higher than 70 mph at higher elevations. EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds bent and downed a commercial radio communication tower 90 degrees at Juniper Flats (7700 ft MSL) in the Mule Mountains. A weather station at about 5400 ft MSL near Bisbee measured a peak gust of 71 mph at 1048MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent strong high pressure aloft created an extended period of very hot temperatures July 14-30 across southeast Arizona. High temperatures were 5 to 14 degrees above normal causing the Major Heat Risk category to be reached and numerous high temperatures to be established. Several dozen heat-related fatalities were reported by the Pima County Medical Examiner.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures of 6 to 11 degrees above normal caused the Major Heat Risk category to be reached across south central and southeast Pinal County July 14-29. Record highs at Picacho Peak Ajo included 113 degrees July 19-21; and 112 on July 25. The hottest temperature was 114 degrees on the 26th. Record highs at San Manuel were 108 degrees on July 25; 107 on July 20 and 26; and 106 on July 15, 17-18, 21, and 27-28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent strong high pressure aloft created an extended period of very hot temperatures July 14-30 across southeast Arizona. High temperatures were 5 to 14 degrees above normal causing the Major Heat Risk category to be reached and numerous high temperatures to be established. Several dozen heat-related fatalities were reported by the Pima County Medical Examiner.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures of 10 to 15 degrees above normal caused the Major Heat Risk category to be reached in Cochise County July 15-22. Record highs included 111 degrees July 17-18, and 110 July 20-21 at Cascabel; 104 July 21-22 at Tombstone; 106 on July 21, and 105 July 18-20 and 22 at Sierra Vista; 107 on July 19, and 106 on July 18, 20-21 at Bisbee-Douglas Airport; 110 on July 21, 109 on July 22, and 108 on July 16 at Willcox; 107 on July 17 and 20 at San Simon. The Pima County Medical Examiner determined that heat was the primary cause of death for one individual on July 16 whose A/C was turned off in Sierra Vista and for another who was found in the desert near the International Border and New Mexico state line on the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent strong high pressure aloft created an extended period of very hot temperatures July 14-30 across southeast Arizona. High temperatures were 5 to 14 degrees above normal causing the Major Heat Risk category to be reached and numerous high temperatures to be established. Several dozen heat-related fatalities were reported by the Pima County Medical Examiner.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures in the upper 90s and 5-9 degrees above normal caused the Moderate Heat Risk category to be reached in Bisbee. The Pima County Medical Examiner determined heat to be a contributing factor in the death of one individual with preexisting health conditions who died in their home due to a nonfunctioning A/C unit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and moved northwest across southeast Arizona through the evening. Storms produced damaging winds and large hail across eastern Pima County and the Tohono O'odham Nation and a dust storm in Pinal County. The Catalina Foothills were especially hit hard with numerous power poles downed leaving thousands without power for two days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow winds caused a wall of dust to travel northwest across south central Pinal County. ADOT sensors detected blowing dust reducing visibilities to less than a quarter mile along I-10 from Picacho Peak to Eloy.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SANTA CRUZ RIVER AND ALTAR VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft strengthened over Arizona and created a period of very hot temperatures August 4-6 across southeast Arizona. High temperatures were 8 to 13 degrees above normal causing the Major Heat Risk category to be reached and numerous high temperature records to be established. A total of nine heat-related fatalities were reported by the Pima County Medical Examiner.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures of 8 to 13 degrees above normal caused the Major Heat Risk category to be reached across the Tohono O'odham Nation, southeast Pima County, and  the lower elevations of Santa Cruz County August 4-6. Record highs at Sells included 109 degrees on August 4 and 5; and 107 at Nogales on August 5. The Pima County Medical Examiner determined that heat was the primary cause of death for four individuals found in the desert during the period; one northwest of Tubac on the 4th and three others on the Tohono O'odham Nation on the 5th in the Sells and Topawa areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft strengthened over Arizona and created a period of very hot temperatures August 4-6 across southeast Arizona. High temperatures were 8 to 13 degrees above normal causing the Major Heat Risk category to be reached and numerous high temperature records to be established. A total of nine heat-related fatalities were reported by the Pima County Medical Examiner.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very hot temperatures of 8 to 11 degrees above normal caused the Major Heat Risk category to be reached across the Tucson Metro area August 4-6. Record highs at Tucson International Airport included 109 degrees on August 4 and 110 on August 5. The Pima County Medical Examiner determined that heat was the primary cause of death for one individual who was walking outdoors on the 5th. Heat was found to be a contributing factor in the death of four individuals. Two were found outdoors and also experienced a complicating factor of drug toxicity. Two were found indoors with a nonfunctioning or nonexistent AC unit and had complicating factors of drug toxicity or another health condition.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms moved northwest across parts of Graham, Cochise, Pinal and Pima counties during the afternoon and evening. Sporadic wind damage and isolated flash flooding occurred in the Tucson Metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain of 1-2 in less than an hour caused the closure of sections of Mission Rd. from Drexel to Campus Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry southwesterly flow aloft with a strong ridge of high pressure to the east over the Southern Plains led to temperatures climbing near or in excess of 110 degrees in Phoenix at the end of June. The hottest temperature in Phoenix for the month was recorded on the 26th with a high of 112 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, seven heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two direct fatalities were reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge over the western United States resulted in high temperatures climbing to around 5-10 degrees above normal in Phoenix during the middle part of May. High temperatures recorded at Phoenix Sky Harbor ranged between 100-105 degrees between the 13th through 16th. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, seven heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two direct fatalities were reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shortwave ridging over the region ahead of an approaching upper level low resulted in afternoon temperatures in Phoenix climbing upwards of 5 degrees above normal in early June. The warmest day in Phoenix during this time occurred on the 5th with a recorded high of 107 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, three heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One indirect fatality was reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure over the western United States led to temperatures climbing in excess of 10 degrees above normal in Phoenix at the end of April. On the 30th, a high temperature of 102 degrees was recorded in Phoenix, tying the record high for the date last set in 1943. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, three heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One direct fatality was reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

CZ_NAME: WHITE MOUNTAINS OF GRAHAM AND GREENLEE COUNTIES
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved through southeast Arizona delivering snow to some of the mountain tops generally above 8000 to 8500 feet. 14 inches of snow was recorded at the Hannagan Meadow SNOTEL but there were no discernable impacts from this event given the elevation that the snow occurred at.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 14 inches of snow at the Hannagan Meadow SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The extreme (D3) drought category continued across far southern and eastern sections of southeast Arizona during December. The area near the New Mexico line encompassed southern Greenlee, southeast Graham and eastern Cochise counties, including the Chiricahua Mountains; while areas near the International Border stretched from the Huachuca Mountains westward across Santa Cruz County to the Buenos Aires National Wildlife Refuge in southeast Pima County. Rain did occur on several occasions in December, though the bulk of it occurred around December 22. December monthly totals ranged from 0.5 to 2.5, with the highest amounts from Tucson westward. In the areas of extreme drought, rain amounts were generally 25 to 75 percent of normal. Soil moisture readings was in the 10th to 20th percentile ranking in most areas through December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper-level low pressure system situated across southern California transported a plume of deep subtropical moisture across the Desert Southwest, with PWAT values peaking as high as 1-1.2 across portions of southern Arizona. PWATs this high in late December is near the 99th percentile of climatology. The anonymously high moisture content in combination with the strong dynamics associated with the low pressure system itself resulted in the development of widespread rainfall activity across most of central and southern Arizona. Widespread rainfall amounts of between 0.5-2 with locally higher amounts were observed, resulting in areas of localized flooding. Embedded within the widespread rainfall activity were a couple of linear segments of strong thunderstorms that materialized mainly across portions of southwestern Arizona between Yuma through western Maricopa Counties due to some instability with MLCAPE values between 100-500 J/KG. Localized damaging winds were observed with a linear segment that moved from west to east from eastern Yuma through southwestern Maricopa Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Oatman RAWS station measured a 66 mph wind gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power pole snapped and multiple power lines down along N Avenue 64 E in Dateland. Winds were estimated at between 75-85 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of moist boundary layer conditions due to rainfall activity 36-48 hours prior and clear skies and near calm winds resulted in the development of widespread dense fog conditions during the early through mid-morning hours on the 24th. Many areas in the Phoenix metro area as well as southward into Pinal County reported visibilities of a quarter mile or less. Phoenix Sky Harbor Airport at one point had to perform a ground stop due to the low visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of less than a quarter mile observed at the Ak-Chin Regional Airport in Casa Grande due to dense fog.

Many areas in the Phoenix metro area as well as southward into Pinal County reported visibilities of a quarter mile or less. Phoenix Sky Harbor Airport at one point had to perform a ground stop due to the low visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility of less than a quarter mile observed at the Casa Grande Municipal Airport due to dense fog.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper-level low pressure system situated across southern California transported a plume of deep subtropical moisture across the Desert Southwest, with PWAT values peaking as high as 1-1.2 across portions of southern Arizona. PWATs this high in late December is near the 99th percentile of climatology. The anonymously high moisture content in combination with the strong dynamics associated with the low pressure system itself resulted in the development of widespread rainfall activity across most of central and southern Arizona. Widespread rainfall amounts of between 0.5-2 with locally higher amounts were observed, resulting in areas of localized flooding. Embedded within the widespread rainfall activity were a couple of linear segments of strong thunderstorms that materialized mainly across portions of southwestern Arizona between Yuma through western Maricopa Counties due to some instability with MLCAPE values between 100-500 J/KG. Localized damaging winds were observed with a linear segment that moved from west to east from eastern Yuma through southwestern Maricopa Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video evidence showed significant flow along Fortuna Wash near I-8. It is likely the water flowed across East Masterson Avenue and Foothills Boulevard, located about 0.5 mile downstream of I-8, making those roadways impassable. Between 1-1.5 inches of rain was estimated to have fallen in the general area based on doppler radar. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A two wave storm system brought snow to the White Mountains over parts of three days. Higher mountains had significant accumulations of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thirteen inches of new snow fell over higher elevations (9,400 feet) of the White mountains over a 50 hour period. Seven inches fell by 800 AM on December 23 (24 hour total). Alpine (8,000 feet) received 7 inches of snow from December 22 to 23.

CZ_NAME: UPPER SANTA CRUZ RIVER AND ALTAR VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The extreme (D3) drought category continued across far southern and eastern sections of southeast Arizona during December. The area near the New Mexico line encompassed southern Greenlee, southeast Graham and eastern Cochise counties, including the Chiricahua Mountains; while areas near the International Border stretched from the Huachuca Mountains westward across Santa Cruz County to the Buenos Aires National Wildlife Refuge in southeast Pima County. Rain did occur on several occasions in December, though the bulk of it occurred around December 22. December monthly totals ranged from 0.5 to 2.5, with the highest amounts from Tucson westward. In the areas of extreme drought, rain amounts were generally 25 to 75 percent of normal. Soil moisture readings was in the 10th to 20th percentile ranking in most areas through December.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed and moved rapidly north across southeast Arizona during the early afternoon. Large hail fell in parts of the Tucson Metro.

A longwave trough moved through the Intermountain West. With synoptic forcing |and moisture still in place over Arizona, scattered showers developed over the area. A developing shower spun up a tornado south of the Mogollon Rim that hit the community of Star Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Survey Summary: Showers developed across the central Mogollon Rim and|moved eastward this morning. Radar indicated that one particular cell|located west-southwest of Star Valley moved quickly to the east-|southeast just after 09:35 AM. NWS Flagstaff, in partnership with the|Hellsgate Fire Department, noted the first sign of damage associated|with the tornado just south of Arizona Route 260, roughly one mile|southwest of Star Valley.||This, in conjunction with radar imagery, provides strong evidence|that the tornado initially developed around 09:39 AM MST. The survey|revealed that the tornado tracked rapidly eastward, consistent with|radar imagery. There was noteworthy damage to a home, an outbuilding,|and additional damage to softwood and hardwood trees during the first|one-fourth to one-third mile portion of the damage path.||The tornado continued for a mile and churned eastward toward|Houston Creek where the damage to residential structures, largely|manufactured homes, was most concentrated. At least 20 structures were|impacted, with damage confined to windows and roofs. Some roofs were|partially removed. The concentration of damage diminished just under|one-half mile east of Houston Creek, suggesting that the tornado|dissipated. This is corroborated with radar imagery, and the estimated time|of dissipation is set at 09:49 AM MST. Based on damage indicators, the|tornado estimated peak wind speed was placed at 105 mph (EF1).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first in a series of low pressure systems brought locally heavy snow to the mountains of far northeast Arizona November 29 to November 30.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of snow fell around Del Muerto (8 miles E-NE of Chinle) at 6,400 feet.  Six inches of snow fell in Chinle at 5,500 feet. Six inches of snow also fell in Many Farms at 5,300 feet elevation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt runoff from the anomalous amounts of snow over the Arizona high terrain led to continued elevated flows in the Salt and Verde River watersheds. Dam releases being conducted in these watersheds to deal with the abnormal runoff led to continued downstream impacts along the Salt and Gila Rivers, which initially began in March. Continued releases from Granite Reef Dam allowed for elevated flows to persist in the normally dry Salt River with multiple unbridged river crossings remaining closed due to flooding. The heightened flows in the Salt River led to the fatalities of two men who were caught in an undercurrent while paddle boarding down the river. Releases from Granite Reef Dam were significantly reduced by the end of April as runoff into the watersheds decreased. Flow in the Salt and Gila Rivers continued to travel downstream to Painted Rock Dam. Releases from this dam led to elevated flows traveling down the Gila River into Yuma County, leading to numerous additional closures of unbridged river crossings. Elevated flows in the Salt and Gila Rivers allowed for impacts to persist into the month of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to the Maricopa County DOT, McKellips Road between Alma School Road and SR 202 remained closed due to flooding from the Salt River beginning on March 7th and continuing through April. Timing based on water releases from Granite Reef Dam.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two men paddle boarding down the Salt River were caught in an undercurrent near 19th Avenue and Broadway on the 9th. Flow in the Salt River was unusually high at the time due to continued upstream releases from Granite Reef Dam. Start time based on continued releases from Granite Reef Dam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep closed low along the California coast and broad upper level ridging centered over the southern Plains promoted deep southerly flow across the Desert Southwest. This southerly flow promoted anomalous moisture advection into the area with PWATs upwards of 1.8-2 in the Lower Colorado River Valley area. Abundant moisture combined with CAPE upwards of 1,500-2,000 J/kg and a weak shortwave embedded in the flow allowed for efficient rain-producing thunderstorms to develop heading into the early afternoon hours on the 2nd. Several rain gages recorded over an inch of rainfall with these thunderstorms across southwest Arizona while radar estimates indicated locally heavier amounts in excess of 1.5 in some areas. As a result of these thunderstorms producing very heavy rainfall amounts in a short period of time, numerous instances of flash flooding occurred, particularly in the Yuma, Quartzsite, and Bouse areas. A couple of water rescues occurred, but no injuries were reported during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quartzsite Fire and Police performed a swift water rescue of two juveniles trapped on a small island in Tyson Wash. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water being released from Painted Rock Dam continued to travel down the Gila River through Yuma County, flooding numerous unbridged river crossings. According to the Yuma County Sheriff's Office, numerous unbridged roads across the Gila River were closed due to flooding going into the last week of April. These road closures persisted into the month of May as elevated flows continued in the Gila River. The USGS gauge along the Gila River near Dateland show the gauge climbing above action stage (8 feet) on April 22nd. By April 30th, the USGS gauge along the Gila River near Dome began to respond as flow in the river continued to gradually travel downstream.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the subtropical high pressure situated across central New Mexico, a southeasterly wind flow was established across Arizona. Thunderstorm activity developed across portions of southeast AZ during the afternoon hours. MLCAPE between 500-1000 J/KG as well as Downdraft CAPE near 2000 J/KG aided in creating a favorable environment for the thunderstorm activity to result in strong downburst winds. The first thunderstorm activity to develop across portions of southeast AZ resulted in the development of a strong outflow boundary, responsible for dense blowing dust conditions across Pinal and southern Maricopa Counties as well as sparking additional storms that affected portions of the Phoenix metro area. With the thunderstorms that moved across the Phoenix metro area, there were several reports of tree damage as well as structural damage to several homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of large trees down in the area of Lehi Road between North Mesa Drive and North Horne in Mesa. One of the tree toppled over a fence. Wind gusts were estimated at 55-60 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low pressure system situated near the northern Baja Peninsula aided in bringing in abnormally high moisture content into south-central and southwest AZ. Precipitable water values of approximately 1 were observed, which for mid-May is a record. MLCAPE values of around 500 J/KG were also observed. The combination of the moisture and instability resulted in the development of an intense cluster of thunderstorms across the higher terrain zones northwest of Phoenix during the afternoon hours, with the activity gradually drifting into northwestern portions of Maricopa County. The activity affected the Wickenburg area, where strong winds and flash flooding due to rainfall just over an inch in one hour were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Running washes lead to the closure of North Vulture Mine Road in Wickenburg. No damages or injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Saguaro tree uprooted in downtown Wickenburg. Winds were estimated at around 50 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two episodes of strong winds, one during the early afternoon hours, most likely due to a dust devil and another one a couple of hours later likely due to the passage of an outflow boundary from thunderstorm activity across the higher terrain areas north and east of Phoenix ripped a carport off of a mobile home located in Mesa, AZ.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Carport was ripped off of a mobile home, located in the area of Broadway and Lindsay Rd in Mesa, during the early afternoon hours, likely due to a dust devil. Awning was then blown back the other direction during the early evening hours likely from an outflow boundary moving through. Winds were estimated at approximately 50 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Water being held in the Painted Rock Reservoir from abnormal winter and early spring runoff continued to be released downstream from April through the month of May. As a result of these continued releases from Painted Rock Dam, downstream impacts along the Gila River between the dam and the Colorado River persisted through the month. Impacts along the Gila River included continued road closures of multiple unbridged crossings. Flow in the Gila River began to drop below Action Stage by the end of the month as releases from Painted Rock Dam decreased. The long duration high flow that occurred in the Gila River resulted in multiple unbridged river crossings being damaged. According to local media reports, Yuma County Public Works reported that 14 unbridged crossings were damaged (1 paved road and 13 dirt roads) due to the flow in the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continued releases from Painted Rock Dam led to prolonged elevated flows traveling down the Gila River. As a result of this, several downstream low water crossings between the dam and the Yuma County line remained impassable due to flooding. This led to the closures of Poco Dinero Road between Painted Rock Dam Road and Saddle Road as well as Rocky Point Road between Hyder Road and Painted Rock Dam Road continuing through the month of May. Any damage sustained to these roads due to the prolonged elevated flow in the Gila River is unknown.

According to local media reports, Yuma County Public Works reported that 14 unbridged crossings were damaged (1 paved road and 13 dirt roads) due to the flow in the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continued releases from Painted Rock Dam led to prolonged elevated flows traveling down the Gila River through Yuma County. As a result, numerous downstream unbridged river crossings in Yuma County remained impassable due to flooding. These unbridged crossings remained closed through the month of May. According to local media reports, Yuma County Public Works reported that 14 unbridged crossings, which include Roll Road (paved) and 13 dirt roads, sustained damage due to the prolonged elevated flows in the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough over the Great Basin gradually moved eastward through Utah into Colorado. As this occurred, a shortwave dropped through southern Utah/northern Arizona late on November 24 into November 25. Southerly flow and increasing pressure gradient,  increased moisture advection into the region. Showers began to develop over southern Utah and southern Nevada during the morning of November 24. The cold front was quite dynamic as it traveled across Arizona with thunderstorms north of Flagstaff with rather decent rainfall rates. The Kaibab Plateau and Chuska Mountains received the greatest amounts of precipitation before the storm moved east on November 25.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow fell at North Rim Entrance Station of the Grand Canyon National Park at 8,800 feet elevation. Four inches of snow fell at the Bright Angel Ranger Station on the North Rim (edge) of the Grand Canyon at just over 8,200 feet elevation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry southwesterly flow aloft over the region with an upper level ridge situated to the east over New Mexico and Texas resulted in temperatures climbing several degrees above normal in Phoenix going into the latter part of June. Temperatures in Phoenix peaked on the 21st and 22nd at 109 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 12 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One direct fatality was reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the subtropical high pressure situated across central New Mexico, a southeasterly wind flow was established across Arizona. Thunderstorm activity developed across portions of southeast AZ during the afternoon hours. MLCAPE between 500-1000 J/KG as well as Downdraft CAPE near 2000 J/KG aided in creating a favorable environment for the thunderstorm activity to result in strong downburst winds. The first thunderstorm activity to develop across portions of southeast AZ resulted in the development of a strong outflow boundary, responsible for dense blowing dust conditions across Pinal and southern Maricopa Counties as well as sparking additional storms that affected portions of the Phoenix metro area. With the thunderstorms that moved across the Phoenix metro area, there were several reports of tree damage as well as structural damage to several homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree down estimated to be 1.5 feet in diameter at the area of West Lakeview Road and North 93rd Avenue in Peoria. Wind gusts were estimated at 55-60 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt runoff from the anomalous amounts of snow over the Arizona high terrain led to continued elevated flows in the Salt and Verde River watersheds. Dam releases being conducted in these watersheds to deal with the abnormal runoff led to continued downstream impacts along the Salt and Gila Rivers, which initially began in March. Continued releases from Granite Reef Dam allowed for elevated flows to persist in the normally dry Salt River with multiple unbridged river crossings remaining closed due to flooding. The heightened flows in the Salt River led to the fatalities of two men who were caught in an undercurrent while paddle boarding down the river. Releases from Granite Reef Dam were significantly reduced by the end of April as runoff into the watersheds decreased. Flow in the Salt and Gila Rivers continued to travel downstream to Painted Rock Dam. Releases from this dam led to elevated flows traveling down the Gila River into Yuma County, leading to numerous additional closures of unbridged river crossings. Elevated flows in the Salt and Gila Rivers allowed for impacts to persist into the month of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to MCDOT, the unbridged Salt River crossing along Gilbert Road between Thomas Road and SR 87 was closed due to flooding beginning on March 14th and continuing into April. The unbridged Gilbert Road crossing sustained heavy damage due to the high flows in the river, keeping the road closed after waters had receded. End timing based on water releases from Granite Reef Dam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm complex known as a Mesoscale Convective System developed across southern Arizona and migrated westward into southern California during the evening and overnight hours on the 30th-31st. The mesoscale convective system developed in an environment characterized by MLCAPE of 1000-1500 J/KG as well as around 30 knots of deep-layer shear from an inverted mid-level trough that was situated over northern Sonora in Mexico. Isolated damaging winds were reported in the Yuma area as well as a lightning induced fire in a tree farm located southwest of the City of Maricopa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large uprooted tree caused a shed to bank upward at Cindy Rose Alterations in the Yuma Foothills. Wind gusts were estimated at between 50-55 mph based on nearby observations. Timing based on radar. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of strong daytime heating and orographic lifting along terrain features led to the development of isolated thunderstorms across south-central AZ during the afternoon hours on the 17th. Moderate instability in place with MLCAPE of approximately 1000 J/KG was sufficient for some of these thunderstorms to become strong as was the case with the storm that developed off the Estrellas Mountains located just southwest of downtown Phoenix and led to a 68 mph measured gust from a mesonet station located at the Phoenix International Raceway in Avondale.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station located at the Phoenix International Raceway in Avondale measured a 68 mph gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm developed near I-10 in Vicksburg Junction in southwest Arizona during the late afternoon hours on the 27th, resulting in multiple tractor trailers being blown over along I-10 between mile posts 41-47. The environment the thunderstorm developed under was characterized by MLCAPE of approximately 500 J/KG along with DCAPE of approximately 2000 J/KG, more than sufficient to result in strong downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately 9 tractor trailer rigs were blown over along I-10 near Vicksburg Rd in Vicksburg Junction. This occurred at two to three locations from milepost 41 to milepost 47 with both directions of traffic affected - especially westbound. Time estimated from first 911 call and radar. Wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph. According to a local news report, five people were hurt with two of them sustaining serious injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an outflow boundary as well as gravity waves in the mid to upper levels originating from a decaying thunderstorm complex that developed across northern Sonora, Mexico the evening prior resulted in elevated thunderstorm development across portions of the Phoenix metro area during the early morning hours of the 8th. Precipitable water values near 1.7 inches and elevated instability with MLCAPE of a couple of hundreds of J/KG were sufficient for some of these thunderstorms to become strong. One thunderstorm complex in particular developed and affected the Chandler to Gilbert areas, resulting in tree damage with estimated wind gusts of between 55-60 mph based on radar data. ||Later during the day, during the afternoon and early evening hours, thunderstorms developed and affected portions of southeast Arizona. Outflows from these storms resulted in additional development further north into northwestern Pinal County, including Arizona City, where some isolated wind damage was reported. The environment was characterized by moderate instability with MLCAPE of 1000-1500 J/KG.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A parked semi-truck was blown over in the area of West Monaco Boulevard and South Saxon Road in Arizona City. Given that the truck was parked, it was likely that it was empty and thus was more likely to be blown over by sub-severe winds, which were estimated to be at around 50 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an outflow boundary as well as gravity waves in the mid to upper levels originating from a decaying thunderstorm complex that developed across northern Sonora, Mexico the evening prior resulted in elevated thunderstorm development across portions of the Phoenix metro area during the early morning hours of the 8th. Precipitable water values near 1.7 inches and elevated instability with MLCAPE of a couple of hundreds of J/KG were sufficient for some of these thunderstorms to become strong. One thunderstorm complex in particular developed and affected the Chandler to Gilbert areas, resulting in tree damage with estimated wind gusts of between 55-60 mph based on radar data. ||Later during the day, during the afternoon and early evening hours, thunderstorms developed and affected portions of southeast Arizona. Outflows from these storms resulted in additional development further north into northwestern Pinal County, including Arizona City, where some isolated wind damage was reported. The environment was characterized by moderate instability with MLCAPE of 1000-1500 J/KG.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video as well as pictures showed several trees were damaged along the general between North Cooper Road and South Gilbert Road in Gilbert. Some trees had tree limbs broken while other trees were uprooted. Wind gusts were estimated between 55-60 mph based on radar estimates. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the subtropical high pressure situated across central New Mexico, a southeasterly wind flow was established across Arizona. Thunderstorm activity developed across portions of southeast AZ during the afternoon hours. MLCAPE between 500-1000 J/KG as well as Downdraft CAPE near 2000 J/KG aided in creating a favorable environment for the thunderstorm activity to result in strong downburst winds. The first thunderstorm activity to develop across portions of southeast AZ resulted in the development of a strong outflow boundary, responsible for dense blowing dust conditions across Pinal and southern Maricopa Counties as well as sparking additional storms that affected portions of the Phoenix metro area. With the thunderstorms that moved across the Phoenix metro area, there were several reports of tree damage as well as structural damage to several homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near zero visibility reported along State Route 347 near mile marker 183 due to dense blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow winds. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While the heaviest rainfall and flash flooding remained to the west across southern California, Tropical Storm Hilary did bring very strong winds to parts of southwest Arizona, which also led to areas of dense blowing dust. Hilary brought strong low level winds upwards of 50-60 knots at 850 mb and 925 mb, which mixed to the surface at times. As a result, very strong winds at the surface led to instances of damage along with low visibility due to blowing dust across Yuma County. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility dropped down to as low as 100 feet at times along I-8 near Tacna due to blowing dust. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility dropped down to 1/4 mile at KNYL. Nearby in the Somerton area, emergency management reported visibility below 1/8 mile due to dense blowing dust. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The overall pattern featured an upper-level low pressure situated along the west coast and the subtropical high pressure centered over New Mexico. This overall pattern configuration resulted in southerly flow across Arizona, which helped transport moisture into the region. Thus, the environment became conducive for thunderstorm activity as PWATs rose to 1.3-1.5 and MLCAPE values ranged between 1000-2000 J/kg across most of central and southern AZ. DCAPE values were very high, on the order of 2000 J/kg, which resulted in a very favorable setup for strong to severe winds. Thunderstorms first developed over southeast Arizona and then, with the southerly flow, were steered into south-central AZ. There were widespread reports of dense blowing dust with visibilities reduced to 1/4 mile or less as well as wind damage as winds were in excess of 60 mph. In addition, a training thunderstorm over northern Maricopa County, in the Anthem area, resulted in 2-4 of rain according to doppler radar estimates and led to significant flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 64 mph was measured at the Ak-Chin Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge of high pressure strengthened over the Intermountain West going into the latter part of August, leading to excessive heat conditions across south-central Arizona. Afternoon high temperatures observed at Phoenix Sky Harbor climbed between 111-115 degrees during the August 15-17th timeframe. On the 16th, the high temperature of 115 degrees tied the record high for the date set back in 2020. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, there were 20 heat-associated fatalities that occurred during this event. Additionally, one heat-associated fatality occurred in La Paz County during this time.

A strong ridge of high pressure situated over the Desert Southwest led to excessive heat conditions across the region to start off July. High temperatures in Phoenix during this time ranged between 111-116 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, there were 10 heat-associated fatalities that occurred during this time. Additionally, one heat-associated fatality also occurred in La Paz County. The month of July would continue on to become the hottest month on record in Phoenix with high temperatures in Phoenix topping out at or above 110 degrees every day except for the 31st. Numerous heat records would be broken and the summer would become the deadliest on record for Phoenix with a total of 645 heat-associated deaths. In addition to the heat fatalities, Phoenix Fire Department responded to a total of 2,240 calls for heat-related illness, which is 36% higher than 2022.

Additionally, six heat-associated fatalities occurred in La Paz County and one heat-associated fatality occurred in southern Gila County during this time.

No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong ridge of high pressure strengthened over the Desert Southwest during the middle part of the month, resulting in well above normal temperatures and excessive heat conditions. Afternoon high temperatures in Phoenix during this time period ranged between 111-119 degrees, tying or breaking daily record highs on the 13th through the 15th as well as the 17th through the 20th. Record warm low temperatures were also set on the 12th through the 14th and the 16th through the 22nd. The warmest low temperature occurred on the 19th when Phoenix Sky Harbor only got down to 97 degrees, breaking the previous all-time record warm low of 96 degrees set in 2003. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 232 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this heat wave. One of the fatalities that occurred during this time include an elderly man who succumbed to the heat on the 16th in the Sun City Festival area after experiencing a flat tire during his bike ride. During the middle of July, morgue capacity quickly went from 64% to 94% due to the heat, resulting in temporary morgue operations being set up. Maricopa County daily heat related emergency department visits peaked on July 19th at 147. In addition to the heat fatalities that occurred in Maricopa County, seven heat-associated fatalities were also reported in La Paz County during this time.

In addition to the heat fatalities that occurred in Maricopa County, seven heat-associated fatalities were also reported in La Paz County during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 73-year-old man went on a bike ride in the Sun City Festival area around 1130MST on the 16th before experiencing a flat tire about an hour into his ride. He then called his wife and agreed to walk and meet her at a nearby fire department on Wagner Complex Drive so she could pick him up. His wife then called 911 after he didn't show up. Police and Buckeye Fire Department searched for the man and found his body in the desert less than a mile from the meeting location where he was planning to meet his wife. The Maricopa County Office of the Medical Examiner determined that his death was due to the heat. Temperatures during the early afternoon hours in this area were around 107-110 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure situated over the Desert Southwest led to excessive heat conditions across the region to start off July. High temperatures in Phoenix during this time ranged between 111-116 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, there were 10 heat-associated fatalities that occurred during this time. Additionally, one heat-associated fatality also occurred in La Paz County. The month of July would continue on to become the hottest month on record in Phoenix with high temperatures in Phoenix topping out at or above 110 degrees every day except for the 31st. Numerous heat records would be broken and the summer would become the deadliest on record for Phoenix with a total of 645 heat-associated deaths. In addition to the heat fatalities, Phoenix Fire Department responded to a total of 2,240 calls for heat-related illness, which is 36% higher than 2022.

A persistent strong ridge of high pressure over the region led to well above normal temperatures for the end of July. Afternoon highs during this time exceeded 110 degrees each day in Phoenix with the 25th recording a high of 119 degrees. Record high temperatures were set on the 24-26th and the 29th. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 103 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time. Additionally, six heat-associated fatalities occurred in La Paz County and one heat-associated fatality occurred in southern Gila County during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 46 heat-associated fatalities (17 direct and 29 indirect) were reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The overall pattern featured an upper-level low pressure situated along the west coast and the subtropical high pressure centered over New Mexico. This overall pattern configuration resulted in southerly flow across Arizona, which helped transport moisture into the region. Thus, the environment became conducive for thunderstorm activity as PWATs rose to 1.3-1.5 and MLCAPE values ranged between 1000-2000 J/kg across most of central and southern AZ. DCAPE values were very high, on the order of 2000 J/kg, which resulted in a very favorable setup for strong to severe winds. Thunderstorms first developed over southeast Arizona and then, with the southerly flow, were steered into south-central AZ. There were widespread reports of dense blowing dust with visibilities reduced to 1/4 mile or less as well as wind damage as winds were in excess of 60 mph. In addition, a training thunderstorm over northern Maricopa County, in the Anthem area, resulted in 2-4 of rain according to doppler radar estimates and led to significant flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 59 mph wind gust was measured at a mesonet station located in the area of North Highland Drive and East Lamar Road in Paradise Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While the heaviest rainfall and flash flooding remained to the west across southern California, Tropical Storm Hilary did bring very strong winds to parts of southwest Arizona, which also led to areas of dense blowing dust. Hilary brought strong low level winds upwards of 50-60 knots at 850 mb and 925 mb, which mixed to the surface at times. As a result, very strong winds at the surface led to instances of damage along with low visibility due to blowing dust across Yuma County. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple instances of wind damage were reported along several different roads near the Tacna area, including Avenue 16, Avenue 31, Avenue 36, and Avenue 42. Along Avenues 31 and 36, railroad crossing signals were toppled or damaged. Power lines were reported down along Avenues 16 and 42. Awnings were also destroyed on RVs in the Welton area. Timing estimated based on nearby observations. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge of high pressure strengthened over the Intermountain West going into the latter part of August, leading to excessive heat conditions across south-central Arizona. Afternoon high temperatures observed at Phoenix Sky Harbor climbed between 111-115 degrees during the August 15-17th timeframe. On the 16th, the high temperature of 115 degrees tied the record high for the date set back in 2020. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, there were 20 heat-associated fatalities that occurred during this event. Additionally, one heat-associated fatality occurred in La Paz County during this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low pressure system located west of the California coast and the subtropical high pressure located over New Mexico led to a south-southeast steering flow across Arizona. Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours across the higher terrain areas of southeastern Arizona and were steered north-northwestward into the south-central Arizona lower deserts with additional development occurring from outflow boundaries. Moderate instability was in place with MLCAPE values ranging between 500-1000 J/KG.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 70 mph wind gust was measured at the Gila Bend Air Force Auxiliary Field.

The elevated water flow caused the closure of Desert Hills Drive between 19th and 15th Avenues due to flooding. No damages or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge of high pressure strengthened over the Intermountain West going into the latter part of August, leading to excessive heat conditions across south-central Arizona. Afternoon high temperatures observed at Phoenix Sky Harbor climbed between 111-115 degrees during the August 15-17th timeframe. On the 16th, the high temperature of 115 degrees tied the record high for the date set back in 2020. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, there were 20 heat-associated fatalities that occurred during this event. Additionally, one heat-associated fatality occurred in La Paz County during this time.

With the subtropical high pressure situated across central New Mexico, a southeasterly wind flow was established across Arizona. Thunderstorm activity developed across portions of southeast AZ during the afternoon hours. MLCAPE between 500-1000 J/KG as well as Downdraft CAPE near 2000 J/KG aided in creating a favorable environment for the thunderstorm activity to result in strong downburst winds. The first thunderstorm activity to develop across portions of southeast AZ resulted in the development of a strong outflow boundary, responsible for dense blowing dust conditions across Pinal and southern Maricopa Counties as well as sparking additional storms that affected portions of the Phoenix metro area. With the thunderstorms that moved across the Phoenix metro area, there were several reports of tree damage as well as structural damage to several homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large pine tree with an estimated trunk diameter of 2-3 feet was knocked over in an apartment complex located in the are West Thunderbird Road in Phoenix. Wind gusts were estimated at 55-60 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep closed low along the California coast and broad upper level ridging centered over the southern Plains promoted deep southerly flow across the Desert Southwest. This southerly flow promoted anomalous moisture advection into the area with PWATs upwards of 1.8-2 in the Lower Colorado River Valley area. Abundant moisture combined with CAPE upwards of 1,500-2,000 J/kg and a weak shortwave embedded in the flow allowed for efficient rain-producing thunderstorms to develop heading into the early afternoon hours on the 2nd. Several rain gages recorded over an inch of rainfall with these thunderstorms across southwest Arizona while radar estimates indicated locally heavier amounts in excess of 1.5 in some areas. As a result of these thunderstorms producing very heavy rainfall amounts in a short period of time, numerous instances of flash flooding occurred, particularly in the Yuma, Quartzsite, and Bouse areas. A couple of water rescues occurred, but no injuries were reported during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low water crossing through Tyson Wash along Quail Trail Street was inundated by flash flooding, causing the road to be close. Timing was estimated based on the Tyson Wash stream gauge response on Main Street. No damage or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent strong ridge of high pressure over the region led to well above normal temperatures for the end of July. Afternoon highs during this time exceeded 110 degrees each day in Phoenix with the 25th recording a high of 119 degrees. Record high temperatures were set on the 24-26th and the 29th. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 103 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this time. Additionally, six heat-associated fatalities occurred in La Paz County and one heat-associated fatality occurred in southern Gila County during this time.

A strong ridge of high pressure situated over the Desert Southwest led to excessive heat conditions across the region to start off July. High temperatures in Phoenix during this time ranged between 111-116 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, there were 10 heat-associated fatalities that occurred during this time. Additionally, one heat-associated fatality also occurred in La Paz County. The month of July would continue on to become the hottest month on record in Phoenix with high temperatures in Phoenix topping out at or above 110 degrees every day except for the 31st. Numerous heat records would be broken and the summer would become the deadliest on record for Phoenix with a total of 645 heat-associated deaths. In addition to the heat fatalities, Phoenix Fire Department responded to a total of 2,240 calls for heat-related illness, which is 36% higher than 2022.

Strong southeasterly flow aloft promoted moisture advection (PWATs 1.5-1.7) into south-central Arizona along with the needed steering flow to allow thunderstorms across southeast Arizona to progress towards the Phoenix area. Effective bulk wind shear values around 35-40 knots allowed for the development of organized thunderstorms, becoming strong to severe across central and southeast Arizona. MLCAPE during this event was around 500-1,000 J/kg while DCAPE around 1,000 J/kg promoted damaging winds. During the early evening hours on the 21st, a severe thunderstorm produced damaging winds that knocked down multiple trees in Apache Junction. No injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Images and videos showed multiple large trees uprooted at the City Hall in Apache Junction. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep southerly fetch of rich moisture up from the Gulf of California led to scattered to widespread heavy rainfall in portions of Southwest Arizona during the afternoon of the 1st. Moisture values were well above normal for the time of year with precipitable water values analyzed around 2.0-2.2 inches. Favorable dynamic and orographic forcing helped lead to strong updrafts and training of storms over certain terrain areas. Radar estimated rain rates were up to around 2-3 inches per hour with locally higher rates over shorter periods. These high rain rates led to areas of high rainfall accumulation, with some areas of more than 1 inch of rain and isolated areas up to 2.5-3.5 inches, radar estimated. Highest measured rainfall were between 1.5-2.0 inches. This resulted in multiple reports of flash flooding in the region, including flooding of the Gila River in Yuma County. On top of the flooding, the heavy mass loading, strong updrafts, and strong cold pools resulted in strong to severe outflow winds emanating out from the showers and storms. , which led to a few severe wind reports and generated dust storm conditions in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arizona Department of Transportation traffic camera along I-8 nearing the Arizona and California state line in Yuma showed visibility down to 1/4 mile in blowing dust from outflow winds from distant thunderstorm activity with wind gusts estimated at between 40-50 mph. No accidents or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The synoptic pattern was characterized by strong westerly flow aloft situated across most of the Desert Southwest with embedded shortwave energy providing the main source for rising motion. Above normal moisture was present with PWATs in excess of 1.5 across most of central and southwestern AZ. The available moisture in combination with the daytime heating led to the development of moderate instability  with MLCAPE in excess of 1000 J/KG. The strong westerly flow aloft led to strong vertical shear with 0-6 KM shear values ranging between 30-40 knots. All of these factors led to a very favorable environment for the development of strong to severe thunderstorms across portions of south-central AZ. Scattered thunderstorms started to develop by the early afternoon hours of the 12th with multiple rounds continuing into the evening hours. One severe thunderstorm in particular developed near the Buckeye area, became supercellular, and tracked generally eastward affecting areas such as Goodyear, Glendale, Phoenix, Scottsdale, Mesa and Apache Junction, causing extensive straight-line wind damage along its path. Additional thunderstorms developed just east of the Phoenix metro area during the overnight and early morning hours of the 13th, with the main impact being isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spring Creek Wash in Roosevelt was running about 3 feet deep. No damages nor injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep closed low along the California coast and broad upper level ridging centered over the southern Plains promoted deep southerly flow across the Desert Southwest. This southerly flow promoted anomalous moisture advection into the area with PWATs upwards of 1.8-2 in the Lower Colorado River Valley area. Abundant moisture combined with CAPE upwards of 1,500-2,000 J/kg and a weak shortwave embedded in the flow allowed for efficient rain-producing thunderstorms to develop heading into the early afternoon hours on the 2nd. Several rain gages recorded over an inch of rainfall with these thunderstorms across southwest Arizona while radar estimates indicated locally heavier amounts in excess of 1.5 in some areas. As a result of these thunderstorms producing very heavy rainfall amounts in a short period of time, numerous instances of flash flooding occurred, particularly in the Yuma, Quartzsite, and Bouse areas. A couple of water rescues occurred, but no injuries were reported during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low water crossing through Tyson Wash along Kuehn Street was inundated by flash flooding, causing the road to be closed. Timing was estimated based on the stream gauge response located along Main Street. No damage or injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A stream gauge located along Bouse Wash at Plomosa Road initially rose above Action stage around 1415MST as a result of flash flooding. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep southerly fetch of rich moisture up from the Gulf of California led to scattered to widespread heavy rainfall in portions of Southwest Arizona during the afternoon of the 1st. Moisture values were well above normal for the time of year with precipitable water values analyzed around 2.0-2.2 inches. Favorable dynamic and orographic forcing helped lead to strong updrafts and training of storms over certain terrain areas. Radar estimated rain rates were up to around 2-3 inches per hour with locally higher rates over shorter periods. These high rain rates led to areas of high rainfall accumulation, with some areas of more than 1 inch of rain and isolated areas up to 2.5-3.5 inches, radar estimated. Highest measured rainfall were between 1.5-2.0 inches. This resulted in multiple reports of flash flooding in the region, including flooding of the Gila River in Yuma County. On top of the flooding, the heavy mass loading, strong updrafts, and strong cold pools resulted in strong to severe outflow winds emanating out from the showers and storms. , which led to a few severe wind reports and generated dust storm conditions in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Yuma International Airport recorded a visibility of less than 1/4 mile in blowing dust due to outflow winds from distant thunderstorm activity. Winds gusts from the outflow were measured at 40-50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge of high pressure strengthened over the Desert Southwest during the time period between the 6-11th, leading to excessive heat conditions across parts of the region. In Phoenix, record high temperatures were set on the 8th, 9th, and 10th. The hottest temperature recorded during this time occurred on the the 9th when Phoenix Sky Harbor recorded a high of 114 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, there were 12 heat-associated fatalities that occurred during this event.

Scattered thunderstorms started to develop by the early afternoon hours of the 12th with multiple rounds continuing into the evening hours. One severe thunderstorm in particular developed near the Buckeye area, became supercellular, and tracked generally eastward affecting areas such as Goodyear, Glendale, Phoenix, Scottsdale, Mesa and Apache Junction, causing extensive straight-line wind damage along its path. Additional thunderstorms developed just east of the Phoenix metro area during the overnight and early morning hours of the 13th, with the main impact being isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed power lines resulting the closure of North Cox Road between West Randolph Road and West McCartney Road in Casa Grande were reported. Wind gusts were estimated at approximately 55-60 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Desert Southwest continued to remain under the influence of an upper level ridge of high pressure during the July 6-10th time period. One heat-associated fatality also occurred in southern Gila County during this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge of high pressure strengthened over the Desert Southwest during the time period between the 6-11th, leading to excessive heat conditions across parts of the region. In Phoenix, record high temperatures were set on the 8th, 9th, and 10th. The hottest temperature recorded during this time occurred on the the 9th when Phoenix Sky Harbor recorded a high of 114 degrees. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, there were 12 heat-associated fatalities that occurred during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure over the Southern Plains expanded westward into the Desert Southwest, leading to excessive heat conditions across the region. Record high temperatures were set on the 5th (116 degrees) and the 6th (114 degrees) in Phoenix, while record warm low temperatures were set on the 2nd (93 degrees), 3rd (92 degrees), and 7th (91 degrees). According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, there were a total of 55 heat-associated fatalities during this heat event. One of the heat fatalities that occurred during this time include a 34-year-old woman who went hiking at Deem Hills Recreation Area during the morning of the 4th but was later found deceased in a remote area near a trail. Additionally, two heat-associated fatalities occurred in La Paz County during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One direct fatality was reported by the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, with their details, such as age, gender, circumstances, and exact location unavailable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong ridge of high pressure strengthened over the Desert Southwest during the middle part of the month, resulting in well above normal temperatures and excessive heat conditions. Afternoon high temperatures in Phoenix during this time period ranged between 111-119 degrees, tying or breaking daily record highs on the 13th through the 15th as well as the 17th through the 20th. Record warm low temperatures were also set on the 12th through the 14th and the 16th through the 22nd. The warmest low temperature occurred on the 19th when Phoenix Sky Harbor only got down to 97 degrees, breaking the previous all-time record warm low of 96 degrees set in 2003. According to the Maricopa County Department of Public Health, 232 heat-associated fatalities occurred during this heat wave. One of the fatalities that occurred during this time include an elderly man who succumbed to the heat on the 16th in the Sun City Festival area after experiencing a flat tire during his bike ride. During the middle of July, morgue capacity quickly went from 64% to 94% due to the heat, resulting in temporary morgue operations being set up. Maricopa County daily heat related emergency department visits peaked on July 19th at 147. In addition to the heat fatalities that occurred in Maricopa County, seven heat-associated fatalities were also reported in La Paz County during this time.
